title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling
text: Sometimes might want group levels factor, may want different groupings Q_{0} versus \alpha. collapse_levels argument allows specify separate collapsing schemes parameter. Structure: Either Q0 alpha () can omitted keep original levels parameter.
code: collapse_levels = list(   Q0 = list(factor_name = list(new_level = c("old_level1", "old_level2"), ...)),   alpha = list(factor_name = list(new_level = c("old_level1", ...), ...)) )

what: Example: Same collapsing for both parameters
title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling
code:
# Ensure levels to collapse are present in ko_alf$dose # levels(ko_alf$dose) are "0.001", "0.003", "3e-04" fit_collapsed_same <- try(   fit_demand_mixed(     data = ko_alf,     y_var = "y_ll4",     x_var = "x",     id_var = "monkey",     factors = "dose",     collapse_levels = list(       Q0 = list(         dose = list(low_doses = c("3e-04", "0.001"), high_dose = "0.003")       ),       alpha = list(         dose = list(low_doses = c("3e-04", "0.001"), high_dose = "0.003")       )     ),     equation_form = "zben",     nlme_control = quick_nlme_control   ),   silent = TRUE )  if (   !is.null(fit_collapsed_same) &&     !inherits(fit_collapsed_same, "try-error") &&     !is.null(fit_collapsed_same$model) ) {   print(fit_collapsed_same)   cat("
Q0 params:", fit_collapsed_same$param_info$num_params_Q0, "
")   cat("alpha params:", fit_collapsed_same$param_info$num_params_alpha, "
") } else {   cat("Collapsed levels model failed to converge.
") } #> Demand NLME Model Fit ('beezdemand_nlme' object) #> --------------------------------------------------- #>  #> Call: #> fit_demand_mixed(data = ko_alf, y_var = "y_ll4", x_var = "x",  #>     id_var = "monkey", factors = "dose", equation_form = "zben",  #>     collapse_levels = list(Q0 = list(dose = list(low_doses = c("3e-04",  #>         "0.001"), high_dose = "0.003")), alpha = list(dose = list(low_doses = c("3e-04",  #>         "0.001"), high_dose = "0.003"))), nlme_control = quick_nlme_control) #>  #> Equation Form Selected:  zben  #> NLME Model Formula: #> y_ll4 ~ Q0 * exp(-(10^alpha/Q0) * (10^Q0) * x) #> <environment: 0x556c85b2c6f8> #> Fixed Effects Structure (Q0):     ~ dose_Q0  #> Fixed Effects Structure (alpha):  ~ dose_alpha  #> Factors:  dose  #> Interaction Term Included:  FALSE  #> ID Variable for Random Effects:  monkey  #>  #> Start Values Used (Fixed Effects Intercepts): #>   Q0 Intercept (log10 scale):  2.271  #>   alpha Intercept (log10 scale):  -3  #>  #> --- NLME Model Fit Summary (from nlme object) --- #> Nonlinear mixed-effects model fit by maximum likelihood #>   Model: nlme_model_formula_obj  #>   Data: data  #>   Log-likelihood: 10.80738 #>   Fixed: list(Q0 ~ dose_Q0, alpha ~ dose_alpha)  #>            Q0.(Intercept)       Q0.dose_Q0low_doses         alpha.(Intercept)  #>                1.89628442                0.37072017               -4.64112061  #> alpha.dose_alphalow_doses  #>               -0.04341491  #>  #> Random effects: #>  Formula: list(Q0 ~ 1, alpha ~ 1) #>  Level: monkey #>  Structure: Diagonal #>         Q0.(Intercept) alpha.(Intercept)  Residual #> StdDev:   4.402379e-06      2.693143e-06 0.1903097 #>  #> Number of Observations: 45 #> Number of Groups: 3  #>  #> --- Additional Fit Statistics --- #> Log-likelihood:  10.81  #> AIC:  -7.615  #> BIC:  5.032  #> --------------------------------------------------- #>  #> Q0 params: 2  #> alpha params: 2

what: Example: Different collapsing for Q0 and alpha
title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling
text: particularly useful want fine-grained distinctions one parameter . instance, might hypothesize maximum consumption (Q_{0}) varies dose, sensitivity (\alpha) . Note: levels factor collapsed single level parameter, factor automatically removed formula parameter (contributes intercept). message inform occurs.
code:
# Q0: keep 2 collapsed levels (low vs high) # alpha: collapse all to 1 level (intercept only) fit_collapsed_asymmetric <- try(   fit_demand_mixed(     data = ko_alf,     y_var = "y_ll4",     x_var = "x",     id_var = "monkey",     factors = "dose",     collapse_levels = list(       Q0 = list(         dose = list(low_doses = c("3e-04", "0.001"), high_dose = "0.003")       ),       alpha = list(dose = list(all_doses = c("3e-04", "0.001", "0.003")))     ),     equation_form = "zben",     nlme_control = quick_nlme_control   ),   silent = TRUE )  if (   !is.null(fit_collapsed_asymmetric) &&     !inherits(fit_collapsed_asymmetric, "try-error") &&     !is.null(fit_collapsed_asymmetric$model) ) {   print(fit_collapsed_asymmetric)   cat("
Q0 params:", fit_collapsed_asymmetric$param_info$num_params_Q0, "
")   cat(     "alpha params:",     fit_collapsed_asymmetric$param_info$num_params_alpha,     "
"   )   cat(     "
Q0 formula:",     fit_collapsed_asymmetric$formula_details$fixed_effects_formula_str_Q0,     "
"   )   cat(     "alpha formula:",     fit_collapsed_asymmetric$formula_details$fixed_effects_formula_str_alpha,     "
"   ) } else {   cat("Asymmetric collapsed levels model failed to converge.
") } #> Demand NLME Model Fit ('beezdemand_nlme' object) #> --------------------------------------------------- #>  #> Call: #> fit_demand_mixed(data = ko_alf, y_var = "y_ll4", x_var = "x",  #>     id_var = "monkey", factors = "dose", equation_form = "zben",  #>     collapse_levels = list(Q0 = list(dose = list(low_doses = c("3e-04",  #>         "0.001"), high_dose = "0.003")), alpha = list(dose = list(all_doses = c("3e-04",  #>         "0.001", "0.003")))), nlme_control = quick_nlme_control) #>  #> Equation Form Selected:  zben  #> NLME Model Formula: #> y_ll4 ~ Q0 * exp(-(10^alpha/Q0) * (10^Q0) * x) #> <environment: 0x556c87439488> #> Fixed Effects Structure (Q0):     ~ dose_Q0  #> Fixed Effects Structure (alpha):  ~ 1  #> Factors:  dose  #> Interaction Term Included:  FALSE  #> ID Variable for Random Effects:  monkey  #>  #> Start Values Used (Fixed Effects Intercepts): #>   Could not determine Q0/alpha intercepts from start_values_used structure. #>   Full start_values_used vector: #> [1]  2.271  0.000 -3.000 #>  #> --- NLME Model Fit Summary (from nlme object) --- #> Nonlinear mixed-effects model fit by maximum likelihood #>   Model: nlme_model_formula_obj  #>   Data: data  #>   Log-likelihood: 10.73793 #>   Fixed: list(Q0 ~ dose_Q0, alpha ~ 1)  #>      Q0.(Intercept) Q0.dose_Q0low_doses               alpha  #>           1.9041039           0.3572637          -4.6750675  #>  #> Random effects: #>  Formula: list(Q0 ~ 1, alpha ~ 1) #>  Level: monkey #>  Structure: Diagonal #>         Q0.(Intercept)        alpha  Residual #> StdDev:   4.408558e-06 2.697616e-06 0.1906036 #>  #> Number of Observations: 45 #> Number of Groups: 3  #>  #> --- Additional Fit Statistics --- #> Log-likelihood:  10.74  #> AIC:  -9.476  #> BIC:  1.364  #> --------------------------------------------------- #>  #> Q0 params: 2  #> alpha params: 1  #>  #> Q0 formula: ~ dose_Q0  #> alpha formula: ~ 1

what: EMMs and Comparisons with Collapsed Factors
title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling
text: using collapse_levels, get_demand_param_emms() get_demand_comparisons() functions automatically handle asymmetric factor structures. EMMs computed using collapsed levels, comparisons performed multiple levels compare. Differential Collapsing Output: Q0 alpha different numbers factor levels (e.g., Q0 keeps original dose levels alpha collapses fewer groups), EMM output include separate factor columns: Original factor column (e.g., dose) uncollapsed parameter (Q0) Suffixed column (e.g., dose_alpha) collapsed parameter (alpha) results cross-joined table showing combinations Q0 levels alpha levels. example, dose 5 original levels alpha collapses 2 groups, EMM table 10 rows (5 × 2).
code:
# Using the asymmetric collapsed model from above (if it converged) if (   !is.null(fit_collapsed_asymmetric) &&     !inherits(fit_collapsed_asymmetric, "try-error") &&     !is.null(fit_collapsed_asymmetric$model) ) {   cat("--- EMMs with collapsed factors ---
")   # EMMs will show collapsed Q0 levels but single alpha value   collapsed_emms <- get_demand_param_emms(     fit_obj = fit_collapsed_asymmetric,     factors_in_emm = "dose", # Use original factor name     include_ev = TRUE   )   print(collapsed_emms)    cat("
--- Comparisons with collapsed factors ---
")   # Q0 will have comparisons (2 levels), alpha will have none (1 level)   collapsed_comparisons <- get_demand_comparisons(     fit_obj = fit_collapsed_asymmetric,     compare_specs = ~dose, # Use original factor name     params_to_compare = c("Q0", "alpha")   )    cat("
Q0 contrasts (collapsed levels):
")   print(collapsed_comparisons$Q0$contrasts_log10)    cat("
alpha contrasts (intercept-only, no comparisons):
")   print(collapsed_comparisons$alpha$contrasts_log10) } else {   cat("Asymmetric collapsed model not available for EMM demonstration.
") } #> --- EMMs with collapsed factors --- #> # A tibble: 2 × 16 #>   dose      Q0_param_log10 LCL_Q0_param_log10 UCL_Q0_param_log10 Q0_natural #>   <fct>              <dbl>              <dbl>              <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 high_dose           1.90               1.77               2.04       80.2 #> 2 low_doses           2.26               2.16               2.36      183.  #> # ℹ 11 more variables: LCL_Q0_natural <dbl>, UCL_Q0_natural <dbl>, #> #   alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, LCL_alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, #> #   UCL_alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, alpha_natural <dbl>, LCL_alpha_natural <dbl>, #> #   UCL_alpha_natural <dbl>, EV <dbl>, LCL_EV <dbl>, UCL_EV <dbl> #>  #> --- Comparisons with collapsed factors --- #>  #> Q0 contrasts (collapsed levels): #> # A tibble: 1 × 8 #>   contrast_definition   estimate     SE    df lower.CL upper.CL t.ratio  p.value #>   <chr>                    <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 high_dose - low_doses   -0.357 0.0803    40   -0.520   -0.195   -4.45  6.73e-5 #>  #> alpha contrasts (intercept-only, no comparisons): #> # A tibble: 0 × 0

what: Plotting a Two-Factor Model with Faceting
title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling
text: Let’s use fit_two_factors_add (converged: y_ll4 ~ drug + dose). can facet one factor color another.  example attempts use fit_two_factors_add. model didn’t converge (common minimal iterations vignette speed), falls back fit_one_factor_dose adjusts aesthetics. plot show population lines, colored one factor faceted another (two factors available).
code: # Assuming fit_two_factors_add was successfully created earlier # If it failed, this chunk won't produce a plot. active_two_factor_fit <- if (   !is.null(fit_two_factors_add) && !is.null(fit_two_factors_add$model) ) {   fit_two_factors_add } else {   # Fallback to a simpler model if the two-factor one failed in the vignette   if (!is.null(fit_one_factor_dose$model)) fit_one_factor_dose else NULL }  if (   !is.null(active_two_factor_fit$model) &&     !is.null(active_two_factor_fit$param_info$factors) &&     length(active_two_factor_fit$param_info$factors) >= 1 ) {   # Determine factors for aesthetics based on what's in active_two_factor_fit   color_factor <- if ("dose" %in% active_two_factor_fit$param_info$factors) {     "dose"   } else {     NULL   }   facet_factor_name <- if (     "drug" %in% active_two_factor_fit$param_info$factors   ) {     "drug"   } else {     # If only 'dose' is available from fit_one_factor_dose, we can't facet by 'drug'     # So, maybe facet by dose instead, or don't facet.     if (       "dose" %in%         active_two_factor_fit$param_info$factors &&         is.null(color_factor)     ) {       color_factor <- "dose" # color by dose if not faceting by it       NULL # No faceting     } else {       NULL     }   }    facet_formula_plot <- if (!is.null(facet_factor_name)) {     stats::as.formula(paste("~", facet_factor_name))   } else {     NULL   }    plot(     active_two_factor_fit,     inv_fun = ll4_inv,     color_by = color_factor,     # linetype_by = if("dose" %in% active_two_factor_fit$param_info$factors) "dose" else NULL, # Example     facet_formula = facet_formula_plot,     title = "Demand Curves (Population Fit)",     observed_point_alpha = 0.5,     ind_line_alpha = .5   ) } else {   cat(     "A suitable two-factor or one-factor model object not available for this plotting example.\n"   ) }

what: Plotting a Model Fit with equation_form = “simplified”
title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling
text: fit model using equation_form = “simplified” (models raw y), inv_fun typically identity predictions already natural scale. Users can customize returned ggplot object adding layers theme adjustments. instance, add custom axis limits breaks:
code: # Assuming fit_simplified_example converged earlier if (   !is.null(fit_simplified_example) && !is.null(fit_simplified_example$model) ) {   plot(     fit_simplified_example,     inv_fun = identity, # Predictions are already on raw y scale     color_by = "dose",     shape_by = "dose",     title = "Demand Model ('simplified' equation, Raw Y)"   ) } else {   cat("fit_simplified_example model object not available for plotting.\n") } #> fit_simplified_example model object not available for plotting. plot_object +     ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(         limits = c(0, 1000),         breaks = c(0, 100, 500, 1000)     )

title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling
text: function helps interpret factors affect Q_{0} \alpha, providing estimates log10 natural scales, optionally Essential Value (EV). Note collapse_levels: model fit using collapse_levels asymmetric specifications (different collapsing Q0 alpha), EMM functions automatically use appropriate collapsed factor names parameter. still specify original factor name factors_in_emm, output show collapsed levels. parameter one level (intercept-), parameter’s values across rows.
code:
# We'll use a model with factors. # If fit_two_factors_add converged, use it. Otherwise, use fit_one_factor_dose. # For the vignette, let's ensure we use one that is likely available. # If fit_two_factors_add is NULL (failed to converge in example), this will use fit_one_factor_dose emm_model_to_use <- if (   !is.null(fit_two_factors_add) && !is.null(fit_two_factors_add$model) ) {   fit_two_factors_add } else if (!is.null(fit_one_factor_dose$model)) {   fit_one_factor_dose } else {   NULL }  if (!is.null(emm_model_to_use)) {   cat(     "--- EMMs for model with factors:",     paste(emm_model_to_use$param_info$factors, collapse = ", "),     "---
"   )    factors_for_emms <- emm_model_to_use$param_info$factors    demand_emms_output <- get_demand_param_emms(     fit_obj = emm_model_to_use,     factors_in_emm = factors_for_emms, # Use factors from the model     include_ev = TRUE   )   print(demand_emms_output)    cat("
--- EMMs for observed factor combinations only: ---
")   # This is useful if the EMM grid includes combinations not in data   observed_demand_emms <- get_observed_demand_param_emms(     fit_obj = emm_model_to_use,     factors_in_emm = factors_for_emms,     include_ev = TRUE   )   print(observed_demand_emms) } else {   cat(     "No suitable model with factors converged for EMM analysis in the vignette.
"   ) } #> --- EMMs for model with factors: dose --- #> # A tibble: 3 × 16 #>   dose  Q0_param_log10 LCL_Q0_param_log10 UCL_Q0_param_log10 Q0_natural #>   <fct>          <dbl>              <dbl>              <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 3e-04           2.42               2.27               2.56      260.  #> 2 0.001           2.16               2.03               2.28      144.  #> 3 0.003           1.90               1.78               2.02       78.8 #> # ℹ 11 more variables: LCL_Q0_natural <dbl>, UCL_Q0_natural <dbl>, #> #   alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, LCL_alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, #> #   UCL_alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, alpha_natural <dbl>, LCL_alpha_natural <dbl>, #> #   UCL_alpha_natural <dbl>, EV <dbl>, LCL_EV <dbl>, UCL_EV <dbl> #>  #> --- EMMs for observed factor combinations only: --- #> # A tibble: 3 × 16 #>   dose  Q0_param_log10 LCL_Q0_param_log10 UCL_Q0_param_log10 Q0_natural #>   <fct>          <dbl>              <dbl>              <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 3e-04           2.42               2.27               2.56      260.  #> 2 0.001           2.16               2.03               2.28      144.  #> 3 0.003           1.90               1.78               2.02       78.8 #> # ℹ 11 more variables: LCL_Q0_natural <dbl>, UCL_Q0_natural <dbl>, #> #   alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, LCL_alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, #> #   UCL_alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, alpha_natural <dbl>, LCL_alpha_natural <dbl>, #> #   UCL_alpha_natural <dbl>, EV <dbl>, LCL_EV <dbl>, UCL_EV <dbl>

title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling
text: Compare levels factors Q_{0} \alpha. Note collapse_levels: using models fit asymmetric collapse_levels, comparisons performed parameters multiple levels. parameter collapsed single level (intercept-), comparisons parameter empty.
code:
# Using the same emm_model_to_use if (   !is.null(emm_model_to_use) && length(emm_model_to_use$param_info$factors) > 0 ) {   factors_present <- emm_model_to_use$param_info$factors    if ("dose" %in% factors_present) {     cat(       "--- Pairwise comparisons for 'dose' (averaging over other factors if any): ---
"     )     comparisons_dose <- get_demand_comparisons(       fit_obj = emm_model_to_use,       compare_specs = ~dose,       contrast_type = "pairwise",       adjust = "fdr",       report_ratios = TRUE     )     print(comparisons_dose)   }    if (all(c("drug", "dose") %in% factors_present)) {     cat(       "
--- Pairwise comparisons for 'drug' within each level of 'dose': ---
"     )     # EMMs calculated over drug*dose, then contrast drug within each dose     comparisons_drug_by_dose <- get_demand_comparisons(       fit_obj = emm_model_to_use,       compare_specs = ~ drug * dose,       contrast_type = "pairwise",       contrast_by = "dose", # Compare 'drug' levels, holding 'dose' constant       adjust = "fdr",       report_ratios = TRUE     )     print(comparisons_drug_by_dose)   } } else {   cat(     "No suitable model with factors converged for comparisons in the vignette.
"   ) } #> --- Pairwise comparisons for 'dose' (averaging over other factors if any): --- #> Demand Parameter Comparisons (from beezdemand_nlme fit) #> EMMs computed over: ~dose  #> Contrast type: pairwise #> P-value adjustment method: fdr  #> ==================================================

title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling

title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling
text: example shows specify complex random-effects structure (e.g., random slopes) Q_{0} \alpha. Now let’s demonstrate extract individual-level predicted coefficients using get_individual_coefficients() function. combines fixed random effects calculate individual-level parameter estimates subject.

title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling
text: addition factor predictors, can include continuous covariates fixed-effects linear models Q0 alpha. two convenient ways : Add continuous covariates additively using continuous_covariates argument (formula writing required). Specify full right-hand-side using fixed_rhs (formula), gives complete control, including interactions (e.g., factor--continuous). demonstrate , using made-continuous covariate age assigned per subject (monkey).

text: keep single factor (dose) add age additive continuous covariate. RHS becomes ~ 1 + dose + age parameters.

previous_headings: Introduction > Advanced Topics > Continuous Covariates and fixed_rhs
what: B) fixed_rhs with a factor (drug) and a continuous covariate (dose)
title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling
text: specify drug factor treat dose continuous covariate (dose_num, numeric version dose), include age additional continuous covariate. uses shared RHS Q0 alpha: ~ 1 + drug + dose_num + age.

title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling

title: Advanced Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling
text: plot_demand_overlay() superimposes population demand curves multiple models, making easy see different specifications shift predicted curve: Demand curves intercept-dose-factor models overlaid. plot_model_comparison() compares parameter distributions side side: Side--side parameter estimates two NLME models.
code: plot_demand_overlay(   fit_no_factors_alf, fit_one_factor_dose,   labels = c("No factors", "Dose factor"),   inv_fun = ll4_inv ) plot_model_comparison(   fit_no_factors_alf, fit_one_factor_dose,   labels = c("No factors", "Dose factor") )

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: vignette demonstrates perform mixed-effects nonlinear modeling behavioral economic demand data using beezdemand package. focus fit_demand_mixed() function associated helper functions extracting results, making predictions, visualizing fits. models allow individual differences (random effects) examination various factors (fixed effects) influence demand parameters like Q_{0} (maximum consumption zero price) \alpha (sensitivity demand price). parameters Q_{0} \alpha estimated log10 scale numerical stability, reporting functions provide natural, interpretable scale. advanced topics including multi-factor models, collapsing factor levels, estimated marginal means, pairwise comparisons, continuous covariates, complex random effects structures, see vignette("mixed-demand-advanced").

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: examples, use apt ko datasets, assumed available pre-processed. apt dataset contain: id: unique identifier subject. x: price drug. y: consumption drug. ko dataset contain: monkey: subject group identifier random effects. x: price commodity (case fixed-ratio requirement). y: Raw consumption values. typically used simplified exponentiated equation. y_ll4: Consumption, ll4 transformed. typically used zben equation. Factor columns like drug dose.
code: quick_nlme_control <- nlme::nlmeControl(   msMaxIter = 100,   niterEM = 20,   maxIter = 100, # Low iterations for speed   pnlsTol = 0.1,   tolerance = 1e-4, # Looser tolerance   opt = "nlminb",   msVerbose = FALSE )

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: core function fitting nonlinear mixed-effects demand models fit_demand_mixed().

previous_headings: Introduction > Fitting Demand Models with fit_demand_mixed() > APT Fit and Plot
title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
code: apt_ll4 <- apt |>   mutate(y_ll4 = ll4(y))  fit_apt_zben <- fit_demand_mixed(   data = apt_ll4,   y_var = "y_ll4",   x_var = "x",   id_var = "id",   equation_form = "zben",   nlme_control = quick_nlme_control,   start_value_method = "heuristic" ) print(fit_apt_zben) #> Demand NLME Model Fit ('beezdemand_nlme' object) #> --------------------------------------------------- #>  #> Call: #> fit_demand_mixed(data = apt_ll4, y_var = "y_ll4", x_var = "x",  #>     id_var = "id", equation_form = "zben", start_value_method = "heuristic",  #>     nlme_control = quick_nlme_control) #>  #> Equation Form Selected:  zben  #> NLME Model Formula: #> y_ll4 ~ Q0 * exp(-(10^alpha/Q0) * (10^Q0) * x) #> <environment: 0x561cce814568> #> Fixed Effects Structure (Q0 & alpha):  ~ 1  #> Factors: None #> ID Variable for Random Effects:  id  #>  #> Start Values Used (Fixed Effects Intercepts): #>   Q0 Intercept (log10 scale):  0.8117  #>   alpha Intercept (log10 scale):  -3  #>  #> --- NLME Model Fit Summary (from nlme object) --- #> Nonlinear mixed-effects model fit by maximum likelihood #>   Model: nlme_model_formula_obj  #>   Data: data  #>   Log-likelihood: 146.7928 #>   Fixed: list(Q0 ~ 1, alpha ~ 1)  #>         Q0      alpha  #>  0.8578019 -1.9749801  #>  #> Random effects: #>  Formula: list(Q0 ~ 1, alpha ~ 1) #>  Level: id #>  Structure: Diagonal #>                Q0     alpha   Residual #> StdDev: 0.1691518 0.2287771 0.07914016 #>  #> Number of Observations: 160 #> Number of Groups: 10  #>  #> --- Additional Fit Statistics --- #> Log-likelihood:  146.8  #> AIC:  -283.6  #> BIC:  -268.2  #> --------------------------------------------------- plot(   fit_apt_zben,   inv_fun = ll4_inv,   y_trans = "pseudo_log",   x_trans = "pseudo_log",   show_pred_lines = c("population", "individual") ) +   facet_wrap(     ~id   )

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
code: fit_apt_simplified <- fit_demand_mixed(   data = apt_ll4,   y_var = "y",   x_var = "x",   id_var = "id",   equation_form = "simplified",   nlme_control = quick_nlme_control,   start_value_method = "heuristic" ) print(fit_apt_simplified) #> Demand NLME Model Fit ('beezdemand_nlme' object) #> --------------------------------------------------- #>  #> Call: #> fit_demand_mixed(data = apt_ll4, y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id",  #>     equation_form = "simplified", start_value_method = "heuristic",  #>     nlme_control = quick_nlme_control) #>  #> Equation Form Selected:  simplified  #> NLME Model Formula: #> y ~ (10^Q0) * exp(-(10^alpha) * (10^Q0) * x) #> <environment: 0x561cda9d7e48> #> Fixed Effects Structure (Q0 & alpha):  ~ 1  #> Factors: None #> ID Variable for Random Effects:  id  #>  #> Start Values Used (Fixed Effects Intercepts): #>   Q0 Intercept (log10 scale):  0.8129  #>   alpha Intercept (log10 scale):  -3  #>  #> --- NLME Model Fit Summary (from nlme object) --- #> Nonlinear mixed-effects model fit by maximum likelihood #>   Model: nlme_model_formula_obj  #>   Data: data  #>   Log-likelihood: -172.7978 #>   Fixed: list(Q0 ~ 1, alpha ~ 1)  #>         Q0      alpha  #>  0.8218145 -1.7748511  #>  #> Random effects: #>  Formula: list(Q0 ~ 1, alpha ~ 1) #>  Level: id #>  Structure: Diagonal #>                Q0     alpha  Residual #> StdDev: 0.1626688 0.1969346 0.5590275 #>  #> Number of Observations: 160 #> Number of Groups: 10  #>  #> --- Additional Fit Statistics --- #> Log-likelihood:  -172.8  #> AIC:  355.6  #> BIC:  371  #> --------------------------------------------------- plot(   fit_apt_simplified,   x_trans = "pseudo_log",   show_pred_lines = c("population", "individual") ) +   facet_wrap(     ~id   )

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: fit_demand_mixed() also supports Koffarnus et al. (2015) equation via equation_form = "exponentiated". default, scaling constant k computed data range (can also specify directly).
code: fit_apt_exponentiated <- fit_demand_mixed(   data = apt,   y_var = "y",   x_var = "x",   id_var = "id",   equation_form = "exponentiated",   k = NULL,   nlme_control = quick_nlme_control,   start_value_method = "heuristic" ) print(fit_apt_exponentiated) #> Demand NLME Model Fit ('beezdemand_nlme' object) #> --------------------------------------------------- #>  #> Call: #> fit_demand_mixed(data = apt, y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id",  #>     equation_form = "exponentiated", k = NULL, start_value_method = "heuristic",  #>     nlme_control = quick_nlme_control) #>  #> Equation Form Selected:  exponentiated  #> NLME Model Formula: #> y ~ (10^Q0) * 10^(1.5 * (exp(-(10^alpha) * (10^Q0) * x) - 1)) #> <environment: 0x561cd6cbbda0> #> Fixed Effects Structure (Q0 & alpha):  ~ 1  #> Factors: None #> ID Variable for Random Effects:  id  #>  #> Start Values Used (Fixed Effects Intercepts): #>   Q0 Intercept (log10 scale):  0.8129  #>   alpha Intercept (log10 scale):  -3  #>  #> --- NLME Model Fit Summary (from nlme object) --- #> Nonlinear mixed-effects model fit by maximum likelihood #>   Model: nlme_model_formula_obj  #>   Data: data  #>   Log-likelihood: -181.3817 #>   Fixed: list(Q0 ~ 1, alpha ~ 1)  #>         Q0      alpha  #>  0.8334103 -2.2391878  #>  #> Random effects: #>  Formula: list(Q0 ~ 1, alpha ~ 1) #>  Level: id #>  Structure: Diagonal #>                Q0     alpha  Residual #> StdDev: 0.1648661 0.2001946 0.5988898 #>  #> Number of Observations: 160 #> Number of Groups: 10  #>  #> --- Additional Fit Statistics --- #> Log-likelihood:  -181.4  #> AIC:  372.8  #> BIC:  388.1  #> ---------------------------------------------------

Detected 1 potential outliers (|resid| > 3 SD) #>  #> Recommendations: #>   - Investigate outlying observations plot_residuals(fit_apt_zben)$fitted #> NULL

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: loss surface profile plots visualize optimization landscape. NLME versions support two modes: type = "ssr" (default): sum--squared residuals aggregated price-level means — quick visual check fixed-effects landscape type = "marginal": linearized marginal negative log-likelihood integrates random-effects variance — statistically appropriate mixed models preferred assessing identifiability near MLE SSR loss surface aggregated means. Marginal NLL surface accounting random-effects variance. Profile plots show 1D slices (one parameter varied, fixed MLE): SSR profile plots Q0 alpha. Marginal NLL profile plots Q0 alpha.
code: plot_loss_surface(fit_apt_zben) plot_loss_surface(fit_apt_zben, type = "marginal") plot_loss_profile(fit_apt_zben) plot_loss_profile(fit_apt_zben, type = "marginal")

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: model estimates global Q_{0} \alpha parameters random effects subjects. output shows model call, selected equation form, model converged, prints nlme model summary.
code: # Make sure a similar 'fit_no_factors' was created successfully in your environment # For the vignette, let's create one that is more likely to converge quickly # by using only Alfentanil data, which is less complex than the full dataset. ko_alf <- ko[ko$drug == "Alfentanil", ]  fit_no_factors_vignette <- fit_demand_mixed(   data = ko_alf,   y_var = "y_ll4",   x_var = "x",   id_var = "monkey",   equation_form = "zben",   nlme_control = quick_nlme_control, # Use quicker control for vignette   start_value_method = "heuristic" # Heuristic is faster for simple model ) print(fit_no_factors_vignette) #> Demand NLME Model Fit ('beezdemand_nlme' object) #> --------------------------------------------------- #>  #> Call: #> fit_demand_mixed(data = ko_alf, y_var = "y_ll4", x_var = "x",  #>     id_var = "monkey", equation_form = "zben", start_value_method = "heuristic",  #>     nlme_control = quick_nlme_control) #>  #> Equation Form Selected:  zben  #> NLME Model Formula: #> y_ll4 ~ Q0 * exp(-(10^alpha/Q0) * (10^Q0) * x) #> <environment: 0x561cd49b3810> #> Fixed Effects Structure (Q0 & alpha):  ~ 1  #> Factors: None #> ID Variable for Random Effects:  monkey  #>  #> Start Values Used (Fixed Effects Intercepts): #>   Q0 Intercept (log10 scale):  2.271  #>   alpha Intercept (log10 scale):  -3  #>  #> --- NLME Model Fit Summary (from nlme object) --- #> Nonlinear mixed-effects model fit by maximum likelihood #>   Model: nlme_model_formula_obj  #>   Data: data  #>   Log-likelihood: 2.763668 #>   Fixed: list(Q0 ~ 1, alpha ~ 1)  #>        Q0     alpha  #>  2.131113 -4.665222  #>  #> Random effects: #>  Formula: list(Q0 ~ 1, alpha ~ 1) #>  Level: monkey #>  Structure: Diagonal #>                   Q0        alpha  Residual #> StdDev: 5.262806e-06 3.217697e-06 0.2275573 #>  #> Number of Observations: 45 #> Number of Groups: 3  #>  #> --- Additional Fit Statistics --- #> Log-likelihood:  2.764  #> AIC:  4.473  #> BIC:  13.51  #> ---------------------------------------------------

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: Let’s model Q_{0} \alpha varying dose Alfentanil.
code: fit_one_factor_dose <- fit_demand_mixed(   data = ko_alf,   y_var = "y_ll4",   x_var = "x",   id_var = "monkey",   factors = "dose",   equation_form = "zben",   nlme_control = quick_nlme_control,   start_value_method = "heuristic" ) print(fit_one_factor_dose) #> Demand NLME Model Fit ('beezdemand_nlme' object) #> --------------------------------------------------- #>  #> Call: #> fit_demand_mixed(data = ko_alf, y_var = "y_ll4", x_var = "x",  #>     id_var = "monkey", factors = "dose", equation_form = "zben",  #>     start_value_method = "heuristic", nlme_control = quick_nlme_control) #>  #> Equation Form Selected:  zben  #> NLME Model Formula: #> y_ll4 ~ Q0 * exp(-(10^alpha/Q0) * (10^Q0) * x) #> <environment: 0x561ccc854f20> #> Fixed Effects Structure (Q0 & alpha):  ~ dose  #> Factors:  dose  #> Interaction Term Included:  FALSE  #> ID Variable for Random Effects:  monkey  #>  #> Start Values Used (Fixed Effects Intercepts): #>   Q0 Intercept (log10 scale):  2.271  #>   alpha Intercept (log10 scale):  -3  #>  #> --- NLME Model Fit Summary (from nlme object) --- #> Nonlinear mixed-effects model fit by maximum likelihood #>   Model: nlme_model_formula_obj  #>   Data: data  #>   Log-likelihood: 17.90035 #>   Fixed: list(Q0 ~ dose, alpha ~ dose)  #>    Q0.(Intercept)      Q0.dose0.001      Q0.dose0.003 alpha.(Intercept)  #>       2.415349697      -0.257733998      -0.519065274      -4.650854662  #>   alpha.dose0.001   alpha.dose0.003  #>      -0.084081282       0.009734047  #>  #> Random effects: #>  Formula: list(Q0 ~ 1, alpha ~ 1) #>  Level: monkey #>  Structure: Diagonal #>         Q0.(Intercept) alpha.(Intercept)  Residual #> StdDev:   3.762036e-06      2.300115e-06 0.1625574 #>  #> Number of Observations: 45 #> Number of Groups: 3  #>  #> --- Additional Fit Statistics --- #> Log-likelihood:  17.9  #> AIC:  -17.8  #> BIC:  -1.541  #> ---------------------------------------------------

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: model fit, can inspect using several S3 methods.
code: # Summary summary(fit_one_factor_dose) #>  #> Nonlinear Mixed-Effects Demand Model Summary #> ==================================================  #>  #> Model Specification: #>   Equation form: zben  #>   Factors: dose  #>   Interaction: FALSE  #>   ID variable: monkey  #>  #> Data Summary: #>   Subjects: 3  #>   Observations: 45  #>  #> Fixed Effects: #>                       Value Std.Error        DF t-value  p-value     #> Q0.(Intercept)    2.602e+02 4.474e+01 3.700e+01   5.817 1.11e-06 *** #> Q0.dose0.001      5.524e-01 1.264e-01 3.700e+01   4.370 9.68e-05 *** #> Q0.dose0.003      3.026e-01 6.833e-02 3.700e+01   4.429 8.10e-05 *** #> alpha.(Intercept) 2.234e-05 2.695e-06 3.700e+01   8.289 5.86e-10 *** #> alpha.dose0.001   8.240e-01 1.387e-01 3.700e+01   5.941 7.56e-07 *** #> alpha.dose0.003   1.023e+00 1.792e-01 3.700e+01   5.707 1.57e-06 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Random Effects: #> monkey = pdDiag(list(Q0 ~ 1,alpha ~ 1))  #>                   Variance     StdDev       #> Q0.(Intercept)    1.415292e-11 3.762036e-06 #> alpha.(Intercept) 5.290530e-12 2.300115e-06 #> Residual          2.642491e-02 1.625574e-01 #>  #> Residual standard error: 0.1626  #>  #> Model Fit: #>   Log-Likelihood: 17.9  #>   AIC: -17.8  #>   BIC: -1.54  # Fixed effects coef(fit_one_factor_dose, type = "fixed") #>    Q0.(Intercept)      Q0.dose0.001      Q0.dose0.003 alpha.(Intercept)  #>       2.415349697      -0.257733998      -0.519065274      -4.650854662  #>   alpha.dose0.001   alpha.dose0.003  #>      -0.084081282       0.009734047  # Random effects (deviations from fixed) head(coef(fit_one_factor_dose, type = "random")) #>   Q0.(Intercept) alpha.(Intercept) #> A   8.839712e-11     -3.421765e-10 #> B   1.247594e-10      3.349284e-11 #> C  -2.131565e-10      3.086837e-10  # Subject-specific coefficients (fixed + random) head(coef(fit_one_factor_dose, type = "combined")) #>   Q0.(Intercept) Q0.dose0.001 Q0.dose0.003 alpha.(Intercept) alpha.dose0.001 #> A        2.41535    -0.257734   -0.5190653         -4.650855     -0.08408128 #> B        2.41535    -0.257734   -0.5190653         -4.650855     -0.08408128 #> C        2.41535    -0.257734   -0.5190653         -4.650855     -0.08408128 #>   alpha.dose0.003 #> A     0.009734047 #> B     0.009734047 #> C     0.009734047  # Access nlme fixef/ranef directly nlme::fixef(fit_one_factor_dose) #>    Q0.(Intercept)      Q0.dose0.001      Q0.dose0.003 alpha.(Intercept)  #>       2.415349697      -0.257733998      -0.519065274      -4.650854662  #>   alpha.dose0.001   alpha.dose0.003  #>      -0.084081282       0.009734047 utils::head(nlme::ranef(fit_one_factor_dose)) #>   Q0.(Intercept) alpha.(Intercept) #> A   8.839712e-11     -3.421765e-10 #> B   1.247594e-10      3.349284e-11 #> C  -2.131565e-10      3.086837e-10  # Start values that were used for the NLME fit fit_one_factor_dose$start_values_used #> [1]  2.270679  0.000000  0.000000 -3.000000  0.000000  0.000000

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: S3 predict() method can generate population-level group-level predictions.
code: # Population-level predictions (log10 scale for 'zben') preds_pop_log <- predict(fit_one_factor_dose, level = 0) head(preds_pop_log) #> # A tibble: 6 × 7 #>   monkey     x     y y_ll4 drug       dose  .fitted #>   <fct>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <fct>      <fct>   <dbl> #> 1 A         10 120.  2.08  Alfentanil 0.003   1.88  #> 2 A         32 110   2.04  Alfentanil 0.003   1.84  #> 3 A        100  66.5 1.82  Alfentanil 0.003   1.72  #> 4 A        320  39   1.59  Alfentanil 0.003   1.40  #> 5 A       1000   9.5 0.978 Alfentanil 0.003   0.734 #> 6 B         10  77   1.89  Alfentanil 0.003   1.88  # Population-level predictions (natural scale, back-transformed) preds_pop_natural <- predict(   fit_one_factor_dose,   level = 0,   inv_fun = ll4_inv ) head(preds_pop_natural) #> # A tibble: 6 × 7 #>   monkey     x     y y_ll4 drug       dose  .fitted #>   <fct>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <fct>      <fct>   <dbl> #> 1 A         10 120.  2.08  Alfentanil 0.003   75.6  #> 2 A         32 110   2.04  Alfentanil 0.003   69.1  #> 3 A        100  66.5 1.82  Alfentanil 0.003   53.0  #> 4 A        320  39   1.59  Alfentanil 0.003   25.1  #> 5 A       1000   9.5 0.978 Alfentanil 0.003    5.42 #> 6 B         10  77   1.89  Alfentanil 0.003   75.6  # Group-level predictions for first few data points sample_newdata <- fit_one_factor_dose$data[1:5, ] preds_group_log <- predict(fit_one_factor_dose, newdata = sample_newdata, level = 1) preds_group_log #> # A tibble: 5 × 7 #>   monkey     x     y y_ll4 drug       dose  .fitted #>   <fct>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <fct>      <fct>   <dbl> #> 1 A         10 120.  2.08  Alfentanil 0.003   1.88  #> 2 A         32 110   2.04  Alfentanil 0.003   1.84  #> 3 A        100  66.5 1.82  Alfentanil 0.003   1.72  #> 4 A        320  39   1.59  Alfentanil 0.003   1.40  #> 5 A       1000   9.5 0.978 Alfentanil 0.003   0.734

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: beezdemand package provides S3 plot() method beezdemand_nlme objects, built using ggplot2, help visualize fitted demand curves observed data. Key Features: Observed Data: Can display original data points. Prediction Lines: Plots model-predicted demand curves population level (fixed effects, pred_level = 0) group/subject level (fixed + random effects, pred_level = 1). Inverse Transformation (inv_fun): Allows back-transformation y-axis predictions natural scale (e.g., log10 consumption back raw consumption units). Aesthetic Mapping: Factors can mapped color, linetype (lines), shape (points). Faceting: Supports ggplot2 faceting via facet_formula. Axis Transformations: Allows x_trans y_trans (e.g., “log10”, “pseudo_log”).

previous_headings: Introduction > Visualizing Model Fits with plot()
title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: Let’s use fit_one_factor_dose (modeling demand Alfentanil dose, y_ll4 dependent variable). Demand curves Alfentanil dose (population level). y-axis natural scale. plot shows population-level demand curves dose Alfentanil. y-axis back-transformed natural consumption scale using inv_fun.
code: plot(   fit_one_factor_dose,   inv_fun = ll4_inv,   color_by = "dose",   shape_by = "dose",   observed_point_alpha = 0.7,   title = "Alfentanil Demand by Dose (Population Fit)" )

previous_headings: Introduction > Visualizing Model Fits with plot()
title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: can also visualize individual subject fits setting show_pred_lines = "individual".  , thin line represents fitted curve individual subject (monkey), colored dose.
code: plot(   fit_one_factor_dose,   show_pred_lines = "individual",   inv_fun = ll4_inv,   color_by = "dose",   observed_point_alpha = 0.4,   y_trans = "pseudo_log",   ind_line_alpha = .5,   title = "Alfentanil Demand by Dose (Subject-Specific Fits)" ) +   ggplot2::guides(color = guide_legend(override.aes = list(alpha = 1))) +   facet_grid(~monkey)

previous_headings: Introduction > Visualizing Model Fits with plot()
title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: can use x_trans y_trans axis transformations.  Users can customize returned ggplot object adding layers theme adjustments. instance, add custom axis limits breaks:
code: plot(   fit_one_factor_dose,   inv_fun = ll4_inv,   color_by = "dose",   x_trans = "pseudo_log",   y_trans = "pseudo_log",   title = "Alfentanil Demand (Log10 Price Scale)" ) plot_object +     ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(         limits = c(0, 1000),         breaks = c(0, 100, 500, 1000)     )

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: beezdemand package provides suite tools robustly fitting nonlinear mixed-effects demand models interpreting parameters. parameterizing Q_{0} \alpha log10 scale, numerical stability enhanced, helper functions allow easy back-transformation interpretation natural scale.

title: Mixed-Effects Demand Modeling with `beezdemand`
text: vignette("mixed-demand-advanced") – Multi-factor models, collapsing levels, EMMs, comparisons, covariates, trends vignette("model-selection") – Choosing right model class vignette("fixed-demand") – Fixed-effect demand modeling vignette("hurdle-demand-models") – Two-part hurdle demand models vignette("cross-price-models") – Cross-price demand analysis vignette("group-comparisons") – Group comparisons vignette("migration-guide") – Migrating FitCurves() vignette("beezdemand") – Getting started beezdemand

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: beezdemand package provides multiple approaches fitting behavioral economic demand curves. guide helps choose right modeling approach data research questions.

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: Demand analysis appropriate want : - Quantify value reinforcer (e.g., drugs, food, activities) - Compare demand elasticity across conditions groups - Estimate key parameters like intensity (Q0), elasticity (alpha), breakpoint - Compute derived metrics like Pmax (price maximum expenditure) Omax (maximum expenditure)

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: fitting model, always check data systematic responding. check_systematic_demand() function applies criteria Stein et al. (2015) identify nonsystematic responding patterns including: Trend (DeltaQ): Consumption decrease price increases Bounce: Limited price--price increases consumption Reversals: consumption sustained zeros
code: # Check for systematic demand systematic_check <- check_systematic_demand(apt) head(systematic_check$results) #> # A tibble: 6 × 15 #>   id    type   trend_stat trend_threshold trend_direction trend_pass bounce_stat #>   <chr> <chr>       <dbl>           <dbl> <chr>           <lgl>            <dbl> #> 1 19    demand      0.211           0.025 down            TRUE                 0 #> 2 30    demand      0.144           0.025 down            TRUE                 0 #> 3 38    demand      0.788           0.025 down            TRUE                 0 #> 4 60    demand      0.909           0.025 down            TRUE                 0 #> 5 68    demand      0.909           0.025 down            TRUE                 0 #> 6 106   demand      0.818           0.025 down            TRUE                 0 #> # ℹ 8 more variables: bounce_threshold <dbl>, bounce_direction <chr>, #> #   bounce_pass <lgl>, reversals <int>, reversals_pass <lgl>, returns <int>, #> #   n_positive <int>, systematic <lgl>  # Filter to systematic data only (those that pass all criteria) systematic_ids <- systematic_check$results |>   filter(systematic) |>   dplyr::pull(id)  apt_clean <- apt |>   filter(as.character(id) %in% systematic_ids)  cat("Systematic participants:", n_distinct(apt_clean$id),     "of", n_distinct(apt$id), "\n") #> Systematic participants: 10 of 10

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: data includes grouping variables (gender, condition, etc.), use run checks summaries within group: parameter also works fit_demand_fixed() — see vignette("fixed-demand") full grouped analysis example.
code: data(apt_full)  # Systematicity check by gender sys_by_gender <- check_systematic_demand(apt_full, by = "gender") sys_by_gender #>  #> Systematicity Check (demand) #> ------------------------------  #> Grouped by: gender  #> Total patterns: 1100  #> Systematic: 946 ( 86 %) #> Unsystematic: 154 ( 14 %) #>  #> Use summary() for details, tidy() for per-subject results.  # Descriptive summary by gender desc_by_gender <- get_descriptive_summary(apt_full, by = "gender") desc_by_gender$statistics |> head() #> # A tibble: 6 × 9 #>   gender Price  Mean Median    SD PropZeros   NAs   Min   Max #>   <chr>  <chr> <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> <int> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 Female 0      5.08      5  4.11      0.09     0     0    50 #> 2 Female 0.25   4.77      4  3.97      0.15     0     0    40 #> 3 Female 0.5    4.59      4  3.77      0.16     0     0    30 #> 4 Female 1      4.41      4  3.58      0.16     0     0    20 #> 5 Female 1.5    4.14      4  3.41      0.2      0     0    20 #> 6 Female 2      3.86      4  3.09      0.2      0     0    20

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: Use fit_demand_fixed() want: Individual demand curves participant Quick exploration data Compatibility traditional analysis approaches Per-subject parameter estimates without pooling information

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: Individual demand curves two example participants.
code: # Fit individual demand curves using the Hursh & Silberberg equation fit_fixed <- fit_demand_fixed(   data = apt,   equation = "hs",   k = 2 )  # Print summary print(fit_fixed) #>  #> Fixed-Effect Demand Model #> ========================== #>  #> Call: #> fit_demand_fixed(data = apt, equation = "hs", k = 2) #>  #> Equation: hs  #> k: fixed (2)  #> Subjects: 10 ( 10 converged, 0 failed) #>  #> Use summary() for parameter summaries, tidy() for tidy output.  # Get tidy coefficient table tidy(fit_fixed) |> head() #> # A tibble: 6 × 10 #>   id    term  estimate std.error statistic p.value component estimate_scale #>   <chr> <chr>    <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>     <chr>          #> 1 19    Q0       10.2      0.269        NA      NA fixed     natural        #> 2 30    Q0        2.81     0.226        NA      NA fixed     natural        #> 3 38    Q0        4.50     0.215        NA      NA fixed     natural        #> 4 60    Q0        9.92     0.459        NA      NA fixed     natural        #> 5 68    Q0       10.4      0.329        NA      NA fixed     natural        #> 6 106   Q0        5.68     0.300        NA      NA fixed     natural        #> # ℹ 2 more variables: term_display <chr>, estimate_internal <dbl>  # Get model-level statistics glance(fit_fixed) #> # A tibble: 1 × 12 #>   model_class      backend equation k_spec     nobs n_subjects n_success n_fail #>   <chr>            <chr>   <chr>    <chr>     <int>      <int>     <int>  <int> #> 1 beezdemand_fixed legacy  hs       fixed (2)   146         10        10      0 #> # ℹ 4 more variables: converged <lgl>, logLik <dbl>, AIC <dbl>, BIC <dbl> # Plot individual curves plot(fit_fixed, type = "individual", ids = c("19", "51")) # Basic diagnostics check_demand_model(fit_fixed) #>  #> Model Diagnostics #> ==================================================  #> Model class: beezdemand_fixed  #>  #> Convergence: #>   Status: Converged #>  #> Residuals: #>   Mean: 0.0284 #>   SD: 0.5306 #>   Range: [-1.458, 2.228] #>   Outliers: 3 observations #>  #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Issues Detected (1): #>   1. Detected 3 potential outliers across subjects

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: Use fit_demand_mixed() want: Group comparisons statistical inference Random effects account individual variability Post-hoc comparisons conditions Estimated marginal means factor levels

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: mixed-effects approach zben equation form, transform consumption data using LL4 (log-log 4-parameter adjustment):
code: # Transform consumption using LL4 apt_ll4 <- apt |>   dplyr::mutate(y_ll4 = ll4(y))  # View the transformation apt_ll4 |>   dplyr::filter(id == 19) |>   dplyr::select(id, x, y, y_ll4)

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
code: # Fit mixed-effects model fit_mixed <- fit_demand_mixed(   data = apt_ll4,   y_var = "y_ll4",   x_var = "x",   id_var = "id",   equation_form = "zben" )  # Print summary print(fit_mixed) summary(fit_mixed)  # Basic diagnostics check_demand_model(fit_mixed) plot_residuals(fit_mixed)$fitted

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: experimental designs factors, can use emmeans post-hoc comparisons:
code: # For a model with factors (example with ko dataset): data(ko)  # Prepare data with LL4 transformation # (Note: the ko dataset already includes a y_ll4 column, but we # recreate it here to demonstrate the transformation workflow) ko_ll4 <- ko |>   dplyr::mutate(y_ll4 = ll4(y))  fit <- fit_demand_mixed(   data = ko_ll4,   y_var = "y_ll4",   x_var = "x",   id_var = "monkey",   factors = c("drug", "dose"),   equation_form = "zben" )  # Get estimated marginal means for Q0 and alpha across drug levels emms <- get_demand_param_emms(fit, factors_in_emm = "drug", include_ev = TRUE)  # Pairwise comparisons of drug conditions comps <- get_demand_comparisons(fit, compare_specs = ~drug, contrast_type = "pairwise")

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: Use fit_demand_tmb() want: Group comparisons modern estimation (automatic differentiation) equation choices fit_demand_mixed() (exponential, exponentiated, simplified, zben) Estimated fixed k parameter Robust convergence via multi-start optimization Laplace approximation preferred approach new mixed-effects analyses

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
code: # Fit TMB mixed-effects model fit_tmb <- fit_demand_tmb(   data = apt,   y_var = "y",   x_var = "x",   id_var = "id",   equation = "exponential",   random_effects = c("q0", "alpha") )  # Summary and plot summary(fit_tmb) plot(fit_tmb, type = "demand")  # Subject-level parameters head(get_subject_pars(fit_tmb))

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: comprehensive TMB documentation, see vignette("tmb-mixed-effects").
code: data(apt_full) dat_mf <- apt_full[apt_full$gender %in% c("Male", "Female"), ]  fit_gender <- fit_demand_tmb(   dat_mf,   y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id",   equation = "exponential",   factors = "gender",   random_effects = c("q0", "alpha") )  # Estimated marginal means get_demand_param_emms(fit_gender, param = "Q0")  # Pairwise comparisons get_demand_comparisons(fit_gender, param = "alpha")

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: Use fit_demand_hurdle() : Substantial zero consumption values (>10-15% zeros) Need two-part modeling (zeros vs. positive consumption) Interest participation consumption intensity TMB backend fast, stable estimation

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: hurdle model separates: Part : Probability zero consumption (logistic regression) Part II: Log-consumption given positive response (nonlinear mixed effects)

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
code: # Fit hurdle model with 3 random effects fit_hurdle <- fit_demand_hurdle(   data = apt,   y_var = "y",   x_var = "x",   id_var = "id",   random_effects = c("zeros", "q0", "alpha") )  # Summary summary(fit_hurdle)  # Population demand curve plot(fit_hurdle, type = "demand")  # Probability of zero consumption plot(fit_hurdle, type = "probability")  # Basic diagnostics check_demand_model(fit_hurdle) plot_residuals(fit_hurdle)$fitted plot_qq(fit_hurdle)

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: Compare nested models using likelihood ratio tests:
code: # Fit full model (3 random effects) fit_hurdle3 <- fit_demand_hurdle(   data = apt,   y_var = "y",   x_var = "x",   id_var = "id",   random_effects = c("zeros", "q0", "alpha") )  # Fit simplified model (2 random effects) fit_hurdle2 <- fit_demand_hurdle(   data = apt,   y_var = "y",   x_var = "x",   id_var = "id",   random_effects = c("zeros", "q0")  # No alpha random effect )  # Compare models compare_hurdle_models(fit_hurdle3, fit_hurdle2)  # Unified model comparison (AIC/BIC + LRT when appropriate) compare_models(fit_hurdle3, fit_hurdle2)

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: equation argument determines functional form demand curve. equation trade-offs terms flexibility, zero handling, comparability across studies. Note: "exponential" "hs" refer equation; "exponentiated" "koff" also equivalent. modern names used fit_demand_tmb() fit_demand_hurdle(); legacy names fit_demand_fixed(). Recommendations: new analyses, consider using "simplified" (also called SND) handles zeros natively require specifying k, making results comparable across studies. replication comparability existing literature, use "hs" "koff" k specification original study. using "hs" "koff", zeros consumption data incompatible log transformation dropped warning.

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: scaling constant k controls asymptotic range demand curve: k = 2: Good default purchase task data k = "ind": Calculate k individually participant k = "fit": Estimate k free parameter k = "share": Fit single k shared across participants
code: # Different k specifications fit_k2 <- fit_demand_fixed(apt, k = 2)          # Fixed at 2 fit_kind <- fit_demand_fixed(apt, k = "ind")    # Individual fit_kfit <- fit_demand_fixed(apt, k = "fit")    # Free parameter fit_kshare <- fit_demand_fixed(apt, k = "share") # Shared across participants

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: Problem: Model fails converge produces unreasonable estimates. Solutions: Check data quality check_systematic_demand() Try different starting values Use simpler model (fewer random effects) Ensure sufficient data per participant

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: Problem: Many zeros consumption data. Solutions: Use fit_demand_hurdle() explicitly models zeros fixed-effects: zeros automatically excluded log-transformed equations Consider data quality: excessive zeros may indicate nonsystematic responding

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: Problem: Comparing alpha values different model types. Solution: aware parameterization differences: Hurdle models estimate natural-log scale internally Use tidy(fit, report_space = "log10") comparable output transformation : log10(alpha) = log(alpha) / log(10)

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: Getting Started: See vignette("beezdemand") basic usage Fixed-Effect Models: See vignette("fixed-demand") individual demand curve fitting Group Comparisons: See vignette("group-comparisons") extra sum--squares F-test TMB Mixed-Effects: See vignette("tmb-mixed-effects") modern TMB-based mixed models NLME Mixed Models: See vignette("mixed-demand") NLME-based mixed-effects examples Advanced Mixed Models: See vignette("mixed-demand-advanced") factors, EMMs, covariates Hurdle Models: See vignette("hurdle-demand-models") hurdle model details Cross-Price: See vignette("cross-price-models") substitution analyses Convergence: See vignette("convergence-guide") convergence troubleshooting Migration Guide: See vignette("migration-guide") migrating FitCurves()

title: Choosing the Right Demand Model
text: Hursh, S. R., & Silberberg, . (2008). Economic demand essential value. Psychological Review, 115(1), 186-198. Koffarnus, M. N., Franck, C. T., Stein, J. S., & Bickel, W. K. (2015). modified exponential behavioral economic demand model better describe consumption data. Experimental Clinical Psychopharmacology, 23(6), 504-512. Stein, J. S., Koffarnus, M. N., Snider, S. E., Quisenberry, . J., & Bickel, W. K. (2015). Identification management nonsystematic purchase task data: Toward best practice. Experimental Clinical Psychopharmacology, 23(5), 377-386. Kaplan, B. ., Franck, C. T., McKee, K., Gilroy, S. P., & Koffarnus, M. N. (2021). Applying mixed-effects modeling behavioral economic demand: introduction. Perspectives Behavior Science, 44(2), 333-358. Kristensen, K., Nielsen, ., Berg, C. W., Skaug, H., & Bell, B. M. (2016). TMB: Automatic differentiation Laplace approximation. Journal Statistical Software, 70(5), 1-21. Rzeszutek, M. J., Regnier, S. D., Franck, C. T., & Koffarnus, M. N. (2025). Overviewing exponential model demand introducing simplification solves issues span, scale, zeros. Experimental Clinical Psychopharmacology.

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: fit_demand_tmb() fits continuous mixed-effects demand models using Template Model Builder (TMB). modern alternative fit_demand_mixed() (uses nlme) provides several advantages: Automatic differentiation – exact gradients via compiled C++, replacing numerical finite-difference approximations used nlme Laplace approximation – integrates random effects analytically rather relying iterative linearization Multi-start optimization – automatically tries multiple starting-value sets keeps best fit Four equation forms – exponential, exponentiated, simplified, zero-bounded exponential (zben) Factor covariate support – design matrices group comparisons estimated marginal means (EMMs) pairwise contrasts vignette covers fit_demand_tmb() continuous consumption data. two-part hurdle models explicitly model zero consumption, see vignette("hurdle-demand-models"). individual NLS curves, see vignette("fixed-demand").

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: Population demand curve exponential equation. exponential equation (Hursh & Silberberg, 2008) models log-transformed consumption reliable choice 2-random-effect models. automatically drops zero-consumption observations.
code: fit <- fit_demand_tmb(   dat,   y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id",   equation = "exponential",   random_effects = c("q0", "alpha"),   verbose = 0 ) fit #>  #> TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Model #>  #> Call: #> fit_demand_tmb(data = dat, y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id",  #>     equation = "exponential", random_effects = c("q0", "alpha"),  #>     verbose = 0) #>  #> Equation: exponential  #> Convergence: Yes  #> Number of subjects: 99  #> Number of observations: 1131  #> Observations dropped (zeros): 569  #> Random effects: 2 (q0, alpha)  #> Log-likelihood: -268.54  #> AIC: 551.07  #>  #> Fixed Effects: #>       Q0.0    alpha.0      log_k   logsigma   logsigma logsigma_e    rho_raw  #>     1.6944    -4.5060     0.3399    -0.2940    -0.0952    -1.4906    -0.4812  #>  #> Use summary() for full results. summary(fit) #>  #> TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Model Summary #> ==================================================  #>  #> Equation: exponential  #> Backend: TMB_mixed  #> Convergence: Yes  #> Subjects: 99  Observations: 1131  #>  #> --- Fixed Effects --- #>               term estimate std.error statistic  p.value #>     Q0:(Intercept)   5.4434    0.4171   13.0512  < 2e-16 #>  alpha:(Intercept)   0.0110    0.0012    9.4171  < 2e-16 #>              log_k   0.3399    0.0276   12.2980  < 2e-16 #>           logsigma  -0.2940    0.0748   -3.9297 8.51e-05 #>           logsigma  -0.0952    0.0807   -1.1796 0.238169 #>         logsigma_e  -1.4906    0.0231  -64.4070  < 2e-16 #>            rho_raw  -0.4812    0.1310   -3.6742 0.000239 #>  #> --- Variance Components --- #>              Component Estimate #>     sigma_b (Q0 RE SD)   0.7453 #>  sigma_c (alpha RE SD)   0.9092 #>  sigma_e (Residual SD)   0.2252 #>  #> --- RE Correlations --- #>                      Component Estimate #>  rho_bc (Q0-alpha correlation)  -0.4472 #>  #> --- Fit Statistics --- #> Log-likelihood: -268.54  #> AIC: 551.07  #> BIC: 586.29  #>  #> --- Population Demand Metrics --- #> Pmax: 8.6507  Omax: 12.6853  Method: analytic_lambert_w #>  #> --- Individual Parameter Summaries --- #>   Q0: Min=1.0764  Med=5.8144  Mean=6.9916  Max=27.2637 #>   alpha: Min=0.0012  Med=0.0117  Mean=0.0180  Max=0.1803 #>   Pmax: Min=0.3701  Med=8.9913  Mean=10.5038  Max=54.8223 #>   Omax: Min=0.7770  Med=11.9321  Mean=17.2419  Max=120.2688 #>  #> Notes: #>   * 569 zero-consumption observations dropped for equation='exponential'. plot(fit, type = "demand")

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: fit_demand_tmb() supports four demand equations:

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: Exponential (Hursh & Silberberg, 2008): \log(Q_{ij}) = \log(Q_{0i}) + k \left(e^{-\alpha_i \cdot Q_{0i} \cdot C_j} - 1\right) + \varepsilon_{ij} Exponentiated (Koffarnus et al., 2015): Q_{ij} = Q_{0i} \cdot 10^{k \left(e^{-\alpha_i \cdot Q_{0i} \cdot C_j} - 1\right)} + \varepsilon_{ij} Simplified: Q_{ij} = Q_{0i} \cdot e^{-\alpha_i \cdot Q_{0i} \cdot C_j} + \varepsilon_{ij} Zero-bounded exponential (zben) (see src/MixedDemand.h full specification): \log_{10}(Q_{0i}) \cdot e^{-\frac{\alpha_i}{\log_{10}(Q_{0i})} \cdot Q_{0i} \cdot C_j} + \varepsilon_{ij} Q_{0i} = \exp(\mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}_{Q_0} + b_i), \alpha_i = \exp(\mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}_\alpha + c_i), (b_i, c_i) correlated random effects. Note: AIC values directly comparable across equations model different response scales (log Q vs raw Q vs LL4(Q)). Convergence tip: exponential equation reliable 2-random-effect models. exponentiated equation works well 1-RE can struggle converge 2-RE, especially smaller samples.
code: fit_exp <- fit_demand_tmb(dat, equation = "exponential",                           random_effects = c("q0", "alpha"), verbose = 0) # exponentiated converges reliably with 1-RE fit_expon <- fit_demand_tmb(dat, equation = "exponentiated",                             random_effects = "q0", verbose = 0) fit_simp <- fit_demand_tmb(dat, equation = "simplified",                            random_effects = "q0", verbose = 0) dat$y_ll4 <- ll4(dat$y) fit_zben <- fit_demand_tmb(dat, y_var = "y_ll4", equation = "zben",                            random_effects = "q0", verbose = 0) data.frame(   equation = c("exponential", "exponentiated", "simplified", "zben"),   random_effects = c("2-RE", "1-RE", "1-RE", "1-RE"),   converged = c(fit_exp$converged, fit_expon$converged,                 fit_simp$converged, fit_zben$converged),   AIC = round(c(AIC(fit_exp), AIC(fit_expon),                 AIC(fit_simp), AIC(fit_zben)), 1) ) #>        equation random_effects converged    AIC #> 1   exponential           2-RE      TRUE  551.1 #> 2 exponentiated           1-RE      TRUE 6744.5 #> 3    simplified           1-RE      TRUE 6743.4 #> 4          zben           1-RE      TRUE -893.6

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: fit_demand_tmb() supports two configurations: random_effects = "q0" – 1-RE: random intercept Q_0 ; \alpha constant across subjects random_effects = c("q0", "alpha") – 2-RE: random effects Q_0 \alpha estimated correlation significant LRT p-value substantially lower AIC/BIC 2-RE model indicate subjects differ meaningfully intensity (Q_0) elasticity (\alpha). b_i column random deviation log(Q_0) c_i random deviation log(\alpha) subject.
code: fit_1re <- fit_demand_tmb(dat, equation = "exponential",                           random_effects = "q0", verbose = 0) fit_2re <- fit  # reuse from Quick Start (exponential, 2-RE) compare_models(fit_1re, fit_2re) #>  #> Model Comparison #> ==================================================  #>  #>    Model          Class   Backend nobs df    logLik       AIC       BIC #>  Model_1 beezdemand_tmb TMB_mixed 1131  5 -639.5903 1289.1807 1314.3350 #>  Model_2 beezdemand_tmb TMB_mixed 1131  7 -268.5364  551.0729  586.2889 #>  delta_AIC delta_BIC #>   738.1078  728.0461 #>     0.0000    0.0000 #>  #> Best model by BIC: Model_2  #>  #> Likelihood Ratio Tests: #> ----------------------------------------  #>          Comparison  LR_stat df p_value #>  Model_1 vs Model_2 742.1078  2  <2e-16 #>  #> Notes: #>   - LRT nesting assumption not verified. head(nlme::ranef(fit_2re)) #>   id        b_i         c_i #> 1 16 -0.8553425  0.38606925 #> 2 24  0.0892061 -0.04866633 #> 3 33  0.6114842 -0.35230990 #> 4 40  0.1527007  0.50021504 #> 5 42  0.7378500 -0.89299758 #> 6 49 -0.2956502  0.40088468

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: exponential exponentiated equations, k scales range demand curve. default estimated (estimate_k = TRUE). can fix conventional value 2 (Hursh & Silberberg, 2008): simplified zben equations use k parameter.
code: fit_k_free <- fit_demand_tmb(dat, equation = "exponential",                               random_effects = c("q0", "alpha"),                               estimate_k = TRUE, verbose = 0) fit_k_fixed <- fit_demand_tmb(dat, equation = "exponential",                                random_effects = c("q0", "alpha"),                                estimate_k = FALSE, k = 2, verbose = 0)  data.frame(   k = c("estimated", "fixed at 2"),   converged = c(fit_k_free$converged, fit_k_fixed$converged),   AIC = round(c(AIC(fit_k_free), AIC(fit_k_fixed)), 1),   k_value = round(c(exp(coef(fit_k_free)[["log_k"]]), 2), 3) ) #>            k converged   AIC k_value #> 1  estimated      TRUE 551.1   1.405 #> 2 fixed at 2      TRUE 607.1   2.000

ℹ 2 more variables: term_display <chr>, estimate_internal <dbl> glance(fit_2re) #> # A tibble: 1 × 10 #>   model_class    backend   equation   nobs n_subjects n_random_effects converged #>   <chr>          <chr>     <chr>     <int>      <int>            <int> <lgl>     #> 1 beezdemand_tmb TMB_mixed exponent…  1131         99                2 TRUE      #> # title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: subject gets empirical Bayes estimates Q_0, \alpha, P_{max}, O_{max}:

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: calculate_amplitude_persistence() collapses per-subject parameters two latent factors used behavioral economic indices: Amplitude (intensity demand, primarily Q_0) Persistence (sensitivity price, drawn P_{max}, O_{max}, 1/\alpha). TMB method extracts subject parameters fit$subject_pars delegates default Z-score implementation, result comparable across model tiers. construction, factors sample-standardized (mean 0, SD 1 within fitted dataset). cross-sample comparisons, supply external basis_means basis_sds standardization uses fixed reference.

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: default, confidence intervals internal (log) scale. Use report_space = "natural" back-transformed intervals:
code: confint(fit_2re) #> # A tibble: 7 × 5 #>   term              estimate conf.low conf.high level #>   <chr>                <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 Q0:(Intercept)      1.69      1.54     1.84    0.95 #> 2 alpha:(Intercept)  -4.51     -4.71    -4.30    0.95 #> 3 log_k               0.340     0.286    0.394   0.95 #> 4 logsigma           -0.294    -0.441   -0.147   0.95 #> 5 logsigma           -0.0952   -0.254    0.0630  0.95 #> 6 logsigma_e         -1.49     -1.54    -1.45    0.95 #> 7 rho_raw            -0.481    -0.738   -0.225   0.95 confint(fit_2re, report_space = "natural") #> # A tibble: 7 × 5 #>   term              estimate conf.low conf.high level #>   <chr>                <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 Q0:(Intercept)      5.44    4.68       6.33    0.95 #> 2 alpha:(Intercept)   0.0110  0.00897    0.0136  0.95 #> 3 log_k               1.40    1.33       1.48    0.95 #> 4 logsigma           -0.294  -0.441     -0.147   0.95 #> 5 logsigma           -0.0952 -0.254      0.0630  0.95 #> 6 logsigma_e         -1.49   -1.54      -1.45    0.95 #> 7 rho_raw            -0.481  -0.738     -0.225   0.95

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: Population-level demand curve confidence band. Individual demand curves random sample subjects. Distribution subject-specific demand parameters.

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: fitting, assess model health built-diagnostic tools. fit object exposes hessian_pd field flags whether Hessian returned TMB::sdreport() positive definite. FALSE, standard errors, p-values, Wald confidence intervals derived Hessian treated unreliable; warning also surfaced note summary() output hessian_warning attribute tidy() output. Q-Q plots subject-level random effects. Random effects diagnostic panels. Residuals vs fitted values 2-RE exponential model. diagnostics help identify: Q-Q plots: Non-normality random effects (heavy tails, outliers) check_demand_model(): Convergence issues, boundary estimates, residual patterns Augmented data: Observation-level residuals identifying poorly fitting subjects Residual plots: Heteroscedasticity, non-linearity, outliers fitted vs residual pattern
code: fit_2re$hessian_pd #> [1] TRUE # Model health check — convergence, variance components, residual stats, # and (since 0.3.0) Hessian positive-definiteness. check_demand_model(fit_2re) #>  #> Model Diagnostics #> ==================================================  #> Model class: beezdemand_tmb  #>  #> Convergence: #>   Status: Converged #>  #> Random Effects: #>   sigma_b variance: 0.7453 #>   sigma_c variance: 0.9092 #>  #> Residuals: #>   Mean: -0.0001171 #>   SD: 0.2069 #>   Range: [-0.9401, 0.7515] #>   Outliers: 12 observations #>  #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Issues Detected (1): #>   1. Detected 12 potential outliers (|resid| > 3 SD) #>  #> Recommendations: #>   - Investigate outlying observations # Augment with fitted values and residuals aug <- augment(fit_2re) head(aug[, c("id", "x", "y", ".fitted", ".resid", ".std_resid")]) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   id        x     y .fitted  .resid .std_resid #>   <fct> <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 16     0        2   0.839 -0.146      -0.648 #> 2 16     0.25     2   0.809 -0.116      -0.513 #> 3 16     0.5      2   0.779 -0.0857     -0.380 #> 4 16     1        2   0.720 -0.0266     -0.118 #> 5 16     1.5      2   0.662  0.0315      0.140 #> 6 16     2        2   0.605  0.0884      0.392 # Q-Q plot of random effects plot_qq(fit_2re) # Random effects diagnostic panels plot_re_diagnostics(fit_2re) # Residual plot — standard in every modeling workflow plot_residuals(fit_2re, type = "fitted")

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: beezdemand provides several specialized plots beyond standard plot() method. work beezdemand_tmb object.

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: expenditure curve shows total spending (P \times Q) function price, vertical horizontal reference lines P_{max} O_{max}: Expenditure curve Pmax Omax reference lines. elasticity curve shows responsive demand price changes. dashed line -1 marks unit elasticity — prices threshold produce elastic demand: -price elasticity curve unit elasticity reference.
code: plot_expenditure(fit_2re) plot_elasticity(fit_2re)

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: loss surface visualizes sum--squared-residuals landscape grid (Q_0, \alpha) values, MLE marked. helps assess identifiability — sharp, well-defined minimum indicates good identification: 2D loss surface (SSR) Q0-alpha parameter space. Profile plots show 1D slices loss surface, fixing one parameter MLE varying : 1D loss profiles Q0 alpha.
code: plot_loss_surface(fit_2re) plot_loss_profile(fit_2re)

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: Visualize distribution subject-level \alpha estimates assess heterogeneity price sensitivity: Distribution subject-level alpha (elasticity) estimates.
code: plot_alpha_distribution(fit_2re)

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: Overlay demand curves multiple models visualize different specifications affect predicted demand function: Demand curves 1-RE 2-RE models overlaid. Compare parameter distributions side side: Side--side parameter estimates 1-RE 2-RE models.
code: plot_demand_overlay(fit_1re, fit_2re, labels = c("1-RE", "2-RE")) plot_model_comparison(fit_1re, fit_2re, labels = c("1-RE", "2-RE"))

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: test whether demand parameters differ group, pass factor variables via factors argument. adds fixed effects design matrices Q_0 \alpha.
code: # Filter to Male/Female for a clean two-level comparison dat_mf <- dat |> filter(gender %in% c("Male", "Female"))  fit_gender <- fit_demand_tmb(   dat_mf,   y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id",   equation = "exponential",   factors = "gender",   random_effects = c("q0", "alpha"),   verbose = 0 ) fit_gender #>  #> TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Model #>  #> Call: #> fit_demand_tmb(data = dat_mf, y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id",  #>     equation = "exponential", random_effects = c("q0", "alpha"),  #>     factors = "gender", verbose = 0) #>  #> Equation: exponential  #> Convergence: Yes  #> Number of subjects: 99  #> Number of observations: 1131  #> Observations dropped (zeros): 569  #> Random effects: 2 (q0, alpha)  #> Log-likelihood: -267.38  #> AIC: 552.76  #>  #> Fixed Effects: #>       Q0.0       Q0.1    alpha.0    alpha.1      log_k   logsigma   logsigma  #>     1.6093     0.1941    -4.4026    -0.2493     0.3404    -0.3021    -0.1074  #> logsigma_e    rho_raw  #>    -1.4905    -0.4591  #>  #> Use summary() for full results.

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: EMMs reported natural scale (estimate) log scale (estimate_log). natural-scale estimates represent population-average Q_0 \alpha group.

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: estimate_ratio column gives multiplicative ratio groups natural scale (e.g., ratio 1.34 means Group ’s Q_0 34% higher Group B’s). Factor interaction continuous covariate sections skipped fast render mode. Set BEEZDEMAND_VIGNETTE_MODE=full include .

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: Use compare_models() compare nested TMB models via likelihood ratio test, AIC, BIC: Valid comparisons require models fit data equation response scale. Models different equations (e.g., exponential vs exponentiated) model different responses compared via AIC.
code: compare_models(fit_1re, fit_2re) #>  #> Model Comparison #> ==================================================  #>  #>    Model          Class   Backend nobs df    logLik       AIC       BIC #>  Model_1 beezdemand_tmb TMB_mixed 1131  5 -639.5903 1289.1807 1314.3350 #>  Model_2 beezdemand_tmb TMB_mixed 1131  7 -268.5364  551.0729  586.2889 #>  delta_AIC delta_BIC #>   738.1078  728.0461 #>     0.0000    0.0000 #>  #> Best model by BIC: Model_2  #>  #> Likelihood Ratio Tests: #> ----------------------------------------  #>          Comparison  LR_stat df p_value #>  Model_1 vs Model_2 742.1078  2  <2e-16 #>  #> Notes: #>   - LRT nesting assumption not verified.

title: TMB Mixed-Effects Demand Models
text: model fails converge, try strategies order:
code: # Switch optimizer fit <- fit_demand_tmb(   dat, equation = "exponential",   tmb_control = list(optimizer = "L-BFGS-B"),   verbose = 0 )  # Warm-start from a previous fit fit2 <- fit_demand_tmb(   dat, equation = "exponential",   tmb_control = list(warm_start = fit$opt$par),   verbose = 0 )  # Apply parameter bounds fit3 <- fit_demand_tmb(   dat, equation = "exponential",   tmb_control = list(     lower = c(log_k = -2),     upper = c(log_k = 4)   ),   verbose = 0 )  # Disable multi-start for faster iteration during exploration fit4 <- fit_demand_tmb(   dat, equation = "exponential",   multi_start = FALSE,   verbose = 2 )

title: Systematic Purchase Task Data Checker — CheckUnsystematic
text: dat Dataframe long form. Colums id, x, y. deltaq Numeric vector length equal one. criterion relative change quantity purchased compared. Relative changes quantity purchased criterion flagged. Default value 0.025. bounce Numeric vector length equal one. criterion number price--price increases consumption exceed 25% initial consumption lowest price, expressed relative total number price increments, compared. relative number price--price increases criterion flagged. Default value 0.10. reversals Numeric vector length equal one. criterion number reversals number consecutive (see ncons0) 0s compared. Number reversals criterion flagged. Default value 0. ncons0 Numer consecutive 0s prior positive value used flag reversal. Value can either 1 (relatively conservative) 2 (default; recommended Stein et al., (2015).

title: Systematic Purchase Task Data Checker — CheckUnsystematic
text: Dataframe

title: Systematic Purchase Task Data Checker — CheckUnsystematic
text: function applies 3 criteria proposed Stein et al., (2015) identification nonsystematic purchase task data. three criteria include trend (deltaq), bounce, reversals 0. Also reports number positive consumption values.
