title: ExtraF — ExtraF
text: dat Long form data frame equation "hs" groups NULL . Character vector matching groups groupcol verbose TRUE, prints output including models k User-defined k value; missing attempt find shared k mean emprirical range (log units) compare Specify whether compare alpha Q0. Default alpha idcol column name treated dataset identifier xcol column name treated "x" data ycol column name treated "y" data groupcol column name treated groups start_alpha Optional numeric inform starting value alpha

title: ExtraF — ExtraF
text: List results models

title: ExtraF — ExtraF
text: One alpha better individual alphas?

title: ExtraF — ExtraF
text: Brent Kaplan bkaplan.ku@gmail.com

title: ExtraF — ExtraF
code: ## Compare two groups using equation by Koffarnus et al., 2015 and a fixed k of 2 # \donttest{ apt$group <- NA apt[apt$id %in% sample(unique(apt$id), length(unique(apt$id))/2), "group"] <- "a" apt$group[is.na(apt$group)] <- "b" ExtraF(apt, "koff", k = 2, groupcol = "group") #> Null hypothesis: alpha same for all data sets #> Alternative hypothesis: alpha different for each data set #> Conclusion: fail to reject the null hypothesis #> F(1,156) = 0.7532, p = 0.3868 # }

title: FitCurves — FitCurves
text: Analyzes purchase task data

title: FitCurves — FitCurves
code: FitCurves(   dat,   equation,   k,   agg = NULL,   detailed = FALSE,   xcol = "x",   ycol = "y",   idcol = "id",   groupcol = NULL,   lobound,   hibound,   constrainq0 = NULL,   startq0 = NULL,   startalpha = NULL,   param_space = c("natural", "log10") )

title: FitCurves — FitCurves
text: dat data frame (long form) purchase task data. equation Character vector length one. Accepts either "hs" Hursh Silberberg (2008) "koff" Koffarnus, Franck, Stein, Bickel (2015). k numeric (character) vector length one. Reflects range consumption log10 units. none provided, k calculated based max/min entire sample + .5. k = "ind", k calculated per individual using max/min + .5. k = "fit", k free parameter individual basis. k = "range", k calculated based max/min entire sample + .5. agg Character vector length one accepts either "Mean" "Pooled". NULL (default), data aggregrated appropriately analyzed specified way. detailed TRUE, output 3 element list including (1) dataframe results, (2) list model objects, (3) list individual dataframes used fitting. Default value FALSE, returns dataframe results. xcol column name treated "x" data ycol column name treated "y" data idcol column name treated dataset identifier groupcol column name treated groups lobound Optional. named vector length 2 ("q0", "alpha") 3 ("q0", "k", "alpha"), latter length k = "fit", specifying lower bounds. hibound Optional. named vector length 2 ("q0", "alpha") 3 ("q0", "k", "alpha"), latter length k = "fit", specifying upper bounds. constrainq0 Optional. number used constrain Q0 fitting process. Currently experimental works fixed k value. startq0 Optional. number used start Q0 fitting process. Currently experimental. startalpha Optional. number used start Alpha fitting process. Currently experimental. param_space Character. One "natural" (default) "log10". Specifies whether parameters (Q0, alpha) estimated natural space log10-transformed space.

title: FitCurves — FitCurves
text: detailed == FALSE (default), dataframe results. detailed == TRUE, 3 element list consisting (1) dataframe results, (2) list model objects, (3) list individual dataframes used fitting

title: FitCurves — FitCurves
text: FitCurves() superseded fit_demand_fixed(), provides modern S3 interface standardized methods (summary(), tidy(), glance(), predict()). FitCurves() continue work longer recommended new code. See vignette("migration-guide") migration instructions.

title: FitCurves — FitCurves
text: Brent Kaplan bkaplan.ku@gmail.com Shawn Gilroy shawn.gilroy@temple.edu

title: FitCurves — FitCurves
code: ## Analyze using Hursh & Silberberg, 2008 equation with a k fixed to 2 FitCurves(apt[sample(apt$id, 5), ], "hs", k = 2) #> Warning: `FitCurves()` was deprecated in beezdemand 0.2.0. #> ℹ Please use `fit_demand_fixed()` instead. #> ℹ FitCurves() returns raw data frames; fit_demand_fixed() returns a #>   structured S3 object with summary(), tidy(), glance(), and predict(). #> ℹ See vignette('migration-guide') for migration instructions. #> Error in GetEmpirical(dat): Duplicates found where id = 68. Check data and rerun.

title: Fit Pooled/Mean Curves — FitMeanCurves
text: dat data frame (long form) purchase task data. equation Character vector length one. Accepts either "hs" Hursh Silberberg (2008) "koff" Koffarnus, Franck, Stein, Bickel (2015). k numeric vector length one. Reflects range consumption log10 units. none provided, k calculated based max/min entire sample. k = "fit", k free parameter remq0e TRUE, removes consumption price price == 0. Default value FALSE replfree Optionally replaces price == 0 specified value. Note, fitting using equation == "hs", 0 first price, 0 gets replaced replfree. Default value .01 rem0 TRUE, removes 0s consumption data prior analysis. Default value FALSE. nrepl Number zeros replace replacement value (replnum). Can accept either number "" zeros replaced. Default replace first zero . replnum Value replace zeros. Default .01 plotcurves Boolean whether create plot. TRUE, "plots/" directory created one level working directory. Default FALSE. method Character string length 1. Accepts "Mean" fit mean data "Pooled" fit pooled data indpoints Boolean whether plot individual points gray. Default TRUE. vartext Character vector specifying indices report plots. Valid indices include "Q0d", "Alpha", "Q0e", "EV", "Pmaxe", "Omaxe", "Pmaxd", "Omaxd", "K", "Q0se", "Alphase", "R2", "AbsSS"

what: Augment a beezdemand_fixed Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_fixed
title: Augment a beezdemand_fixed Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_fixed
text: Returns original data fitted values residuals individual demand curve fits. enables easy model diagnostics visualization tidyverse.

title: Augment a beezdemand_fixed Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_fixed
code: # S3 method for class 'beezdemand_fixed' augment(x, newdata = NULL, ...)

title: Augment a beezdemand_fixed Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_fixed
text: x object class beezdemand_fixed. newdata Optional data frame new data prediction. NULL, uses original data model. ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: Augment a beezdemand_fixed Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_fixed
text: tibble containing original data plus: .fitted Fitted demand values response scale .resid Residuals (observed - fitted)

title: Augment a beezdemand_fixed Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_fixed
text: "hs" equation models fitting done log10 scale, fitted values back-transformed natural scale.

title: Augment a beezdemand_fixed Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_fixed
code: # \donttest{ data(apt) fit <- fit_demand_fixed(apt, y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id") augmented <- augment(fit)  # Plot residuals by subject library(ggplot2) ggplot(augmented, aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +   facet_wrap(~id) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed")  # }

what: Augment a beezdemand_hurdle Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_hurdle
title: Augment a beezdemand_hurdle Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_hurdle
text: Returns original data fitted values, residuals, predictions hurdle demand model. enables easy model diagnostics visualization tidyverse.

title: Augment a beezdemand_hurdle Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_hurdle
code: # S3 method for class 'beezdemand_hurdle' augment(x, newdata = NULL, component = c("combined", "continuous"), ...)

title: Augment a beezdemand_hurdle Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_hurdle
text: x object class beezdemand_hurdle. newdata Optional data frame new data prediction. NULL, uses original data model. component Character. residuals compute: "combined" (Default) Randomized quantile residuals (Dunn & Smyth, 1996) assess binary continuous components simultaneously. model correctly specified, exactly N(0,1). "continuous" Log-scale residuals log(y) - mu positive observations (zeros NA). Assesses Part II specification. "binary" returned residuals; use .fitted_prob column observed binary indicators calibration diagnostics. ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: Augment a beezdemand_hurdle Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_hurdle
text: tibble containing original data plus: .fitted Fitted demand values (natural scale) .fitted_link Fitted values log scale (Part II mean) .fitted_prob Predicted probability consumption (1 - P(zero)) .resid Residuals (type depends component; see ) .resid_response Residuals response scale (y - .fitted)

title: Augment a beezdemand_hurdle Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_hurdle
text: hurdle model two components, requiring different diagnostic approaches: Continuous residuals (component = "continuous"): Standard log-scale residuals log(y) - mu observations y > 0. Zeros excluded (NA). Assesses whether lognormal conditional distribution well- specified. Randomized quantile residuals (component = "combined", default): Following Dunn & Smyth (1996), maps observation fitted hurdle CDF standard normal quantile function. model correctly specified, residuals exactly N(0,1) regardless component generated observation. zeros, uniform random variate within [0, P(zero)] breaks ties otherwise create spike QQ plot.

title: Augment a beezdemand_hurdle Model with Fitted Values and Residuals — augment.beezdemand_hurdle
code: # \donttest{ data(apt) fit <- fit_demand_hurdle(apt, y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id") #> Sample size may be too small for reliable estimation. #>   Subjects: 10, Parameters: 12, Recommended minimum: 60 subjects. #>   Consider using more subjects or the simpler 2-RE model. #> Fitting HurdleDemand3RE model... #>   Part II: zhao_exponential #>   Subjects: 10, Observations: 160 #>   Fixed parameters: 12, Random effects per subject: 3 #>   Optimizing... #>   Converged in 81 iterations #>   Computing standard errors... #> Done. Log-likelihood: 32.81 augmented <- augment(fit)  # Plot residuals library(ggplot2) ggplot(augmented, aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed")  # }

what: beezdemand: Behavioral Economic Easy Demand — beezdemand-package
title: beezdemand: Behavioral Economic Easy Demand — beezdemand-package
text: Facilitates many analyses performed studies behavioral economic demand. package supports commonly-used options modeling operant demand including (1) data screening proposed Stein, Koffarnus, Snider, Quisenberry, & Bickel (2015; doi:10.1037/pha0000020 ), (2) fitting models demand linear (Hursh, Raslear, Bauman, & Black, 1989, doi:10.1007/978-94-009-2470-3_22 ), exponential (Hursh & Silberberg, 2008, doi:10.1037/0033-295X.115.1.186 ) modified exponential (Koffarnus, Franck, Stein, & Bickel, 2015, doi:10.1037/pha0000045 ), (3) calculating numerous measures relevant applied behavioral economists (Intensity, Pmax, Omax). Also supports plotting comparing data.

title: beezdemand: Behavioral Economic Easy Demand — beezdemand-package
text: Maintainer: Brent Kaplan bkaplan.ku@gmail.com (ORCID) [copyright holder] contributors: Shawn Gilroy shawn.gilroy@temple.edu [contributor]

what: Calculate Pmax and Omax with Method Reporting and Parameter-Space Safety — beezdemand_calc_pmax_omax
title: Calculate Pmax and Omax with Method Reporting and Parameter-Space Safety — beezdemand_calc_pmax_omax
text: Unified internal engine pmax/omax computation. Supports analytic solutions (Lambert W HS/hurdle, closed-form SND), numerical fallback, observed (row-wise) metrics. Handles parameter-space conversions transparently.

title: Calculate Pmax and Omax with Method Reporting and Parameter-Space Safety — beezdemand_calc_pmax_omax
code: beezdemand_calc_pmax_omax(   model_type = NULL,   params = NULL,   param_scales = NULL,   expenditure_fn = NULL,   demand_fn = NULL,   p_zero_fn = NULL,   price_obs = NULL,   consumption_obs = NULL,   tol = 0.1,   compute_observed = NULL )

title: Calculate Pmax and Omax with Method Reporting and Parameter-Space Safety — beezdemand_calc_pmax_omax
text: model_type Character: "hs", "koff", "hurdle", "hurdle_hs_stdq0", "snd", "simplified", NULL params Named list parameters. Names depend model_type: hs/koff: alpha, q0, k hurdle: alpha, q0, k (note: hurdle uses different formula) hurdle_hs_stdq0: alpha, q0, k (Q0 appears inside exponent) snd/simplified: alpha, q0 param_scales Named list mapping parameter names input scales: "natural", "log", "log10". Default assumes natural. expenditure_fn Optional function E(p) numerical fallback. NULL model_type provided, constructed params. demand_fn Optional function Q(p) elasticity calculation. price_obs Numeric vector observed prices (required observed metrics domain constraints). consumption_obs Numeric vector observed consumption (observed metrics). tol Tolerance unit elasticity check (default 0.1). compute_observed Logical; compute observed metrics? Default TRUE price_obs consumption_obs provided.

title: Calculate Pmax and Omax with Method Reporting and Parameter-Space Safety — beezdemand_calc_pmax_omax
text: list snake_case fields: pmax_model Model-based pmax omax_model Model-based omax q_at_pmax_model Quantity pmax method_model Method used: "analytic_lambert_w", "analytic_snd", "numerical_optimize_observed_domain" domain_model Price domain used computation is_boundary_model Logical; pmax domain boundary? elasticity_at_pmax_model Elasticity evaluated pmax unit_elasticity_pass_model Logical; elasticity near -1? note_model notes model computation pmax_obs Observed pmax omax_obs Observed omax method_obs Method observed: "row_wise_max" tie_break_obs Tie-break rule: "min_price" n_obs_rows Number observation rows n_unique_prices Number unique prices has_duplicate_prices Logical; duplicate prices detected? n_max_ties Number rows achieving omax note_obs Notes observed computation alpha_scale_in Input scale alpha q0_scale_in Input scale Q0 k_scale_in Input scale k (applicable) note_param_space Notes parameter conversions

title: Calculate Pmax and Omax with Method Reporting and Parameter-Space Safety — beezdemand_calc_pmax_omax
code: # \donttest{ result <- beezdemand_calc_pmax_omax(   model_type = "hs",   params = list(alpha = 0.001, q0 = 10, k = 3),   price_obs = c(0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16),   consumption_obs = c(10, 9, 8, 6, 3, 1, 0) ) result$pmax_model #> [1] 17.19203 result$omax_model #> [1] 57.73857 # }

what: Calculate Omax and Pmax for Demand Curves — calc_omax_pmax
title: Calculate Omax and Pmax for Demand Curves — calc_omax_pmax
text: Calculates maximum expenditure (Omax) price maximum expenditure (Pmax) exponential demand model used two-part hurdle model.

title: Calculate Omax and Pmax for Demand Curves — calc_omax_pmax
code: calc_omax_pmax(Q0, k, alpha, price_range = NULL)

title: Calculate Omax and Pmax for Demand Curves — calc_omax_pmax
text: Q0 Intensity parameter (consumption price 0). k Scaling parameter exponential decay. alpha Elasticity parameter (rate decay). price_range Numeric vector length 2 specifying price range search Pmax. Default NULL, uses adaptive range based alpha (approximately 0 10/alpha).

title: Calculate Omax and Pmax for Demand Curves — calc_omax_pmax
text: named list : Pmax Price maximum expenditure Omax Maximum expenditure (price * quantity) Qmax Quantity Pmax

title: Calculate Omax and Pmax for Demand Curves — calc_omax_pmax
text: demand function: $$Q(p) = Q_0 \cdot \exp(k \cdot (\exp(-\alpha \cdot p) - 1))$$ Expenditure E(p) = p * Q(p). Omax maximum E(p) Pmax price maximum occurs. found numerically. search range automatically adjusted based alpha ensure maximum found. small alpha values, Pmax can quite large.

title: Calculate Omax and Pmax for Demand Curves — calc_omax_pmax
code: # Calculate for group-level parameters calc_omax_pmax(Q0 = 10, k = 2, alpha = 0.5) #> Warning: `calc_omax_pmax()` was deprecated in beezdemand 0.2.0. #> ℹ Please use `beezdemand_calc_pmax_omax()` instead. #> Warning: Note: k (2.000) < e (~2.718); the expenditure function has no interior maximum. Returning the maximum over a bounded search interval via numerical optimization. #> $Pmax #> [1] 19.99993 #>  #> $Omax #> [1] 27.06941 #>  #> $Qmax #> [1] 1.353476 #>  #> $note #> [1] "k < e: bounded-range maximum over [0.001, 20.000] via numerical optimization" #>   # With k >= e (~2.718), a local maximum exists calc_omax_pmax(Q0 = 10, k = 3, alpha = 0.5) #> $Pmax #> [1] 1.238123 #>  #> $Omax #> [1] 3.100397 #>  #> $Qmax #> [1] 2.504112 #>

title: Calculate Amplitude and Persistence — calculate_amplitude_persistence
text: fit object class beezdemand_fixed, beezdemand_hurdle, beezdemand_nlme, data.frame. amplitude Character vector column names consider Amplitude factor. function use first column found data. Default c("Intensity", "Q0d", "Q0"). persistence Character vector column names include Persistence factor. Default c("BP0", "Pmaxe", "Omaxe", "Alpha"). found columns used. use_inv_alpha Logical. "Alpha" (variation) present persistence, inverted (1/Alpha) standardization? Default TRUE. strict Logical. TRUE (default), missing metrics, duplicated ids, data integrity problems produce errors instead warnings. min_persistence_components Integer. Minimum number non-missing standardized persistence components required compute Persistence given id. fewer available, Persistence set NA. Default 2. empirical_y_var beezdemand_nlme objects, optional column name fit$data use computing empirical indices (e.g., BP0). important fitted y_var transformed (e.g., log10). NULL, method attempt choose sensible default may error strict mode ambiguous. basis_means Optional named numeric vector means use Z-score standardization. Names must match columns used (e.g., c(Intensity = 10, BP0 = 5)). NULL (default), sample means used. basis_sds Optional named numeric vector standard deviations use Z-score standardization. Names must match columns used. NULL (default), sample SDs used. ... Additional arguments passed methods.

title: Calculate Amplitude and Persistence — calculate_amplitude_persistence
text: data frame original ID calculated Amplitude Persistence scores, along standardized (Z-scored) constituent metrics.

title: Calculate Amplitude and Persistence — calculate_amplitude_persistence
text: function calculates Amplitude Persistence : Extracting relevant demand metrics fit object. Resolving requested metric columns (case-insensitive, limited synonym support common beezdemand outputs). Inverting Alpha requested (1/Alpha). Standardizing (Z-scoring) metrics. basis_means basis_sds provided, used; otherwise, current sample's statistics used. Aggregating Z-scores two latent factors. Amplitude defined variable specified amplitude (typically Intensity/Q0). Persistence defined mean standardized values variables specified persistence (typically Breakpoint, Pmax, Omax, 1/Alpha).

title: Calculate Amplitude and Persistence — calculate_amplitude_persistence
text: beezdemand_fixed: Extracts metrics fit$results. beezdemand_hurdle: Extracts metrics fit$subject_pars. beezdemand_nlme: Calculates subject-specific parameters fixed random effects. Parameters Q0 Alpha assumed log10 scale zben simplified equations converted linear scale. Omax Pmax calculated empirically predictions. Breakpoint calculated empirically raw data.

what: Check for Unsystematic Patterns in Cross-Price Data — check_unsystematic_cp
title: Check for Unsystematic Patterns in Cross-Price Data — check_unsystematic_cp
text: Analyzes whether consumption data shows systematic trends unsystematic patterns ("bounces") respect price. Includes detection zero-value reversal/return sequences allows flexible output based level detail requested. See Rzeszutek et al. (press) details.

title: Check for Unsystematic Patterns in Cross-Price Data — check_unsystematic_cp
code: check_unsystematic_cp(   data,   delta_threshold = 0.025,   bounce_down_threshold = 0.1,   bounce_up_threshold = 0.1,   bounce_none_threshold = 0.1,   rev_zeroes = 2,   ret_nums = 2,   expected_down = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE,   detailed = FALSE )

title: Check for Unsystematic Patterns in Cross-Price Data — check_unsystematic_cp
text: data data frame columns 'x' 'y', 'x' price 'y' consumption. delta_threshold Numeric. Threshold detecting log-scale trends (default 0.025). bounce_down_threshold Numeric. Minimum downward bounce proportion count significant upward trends. bounce_up_threshold Numeric. Minimum upward bounce proportion count significant downward trends. bounce_none_threshold Numeric. Minimum bounce proportion count significant -trend cases. rev_zeroes Integer. Length zero sequences detect reversals (default 2). ret_nums Integer. Length non-zero sequences detect returns (default 2). expected_down Logical. TRUE, suppress reversal detection. verbose Logical. TRUE, print intermediate values (default FALSE). detailed Logical. TRUE, return additional columns including trend/bounce flags.

title: Check for Unsystematic Patterns in Cross-Price Data — check_unsystematic_cp
text: data frame class cp_unsystematic core results: delta_direction Character: '', '', 'none'. bounce_direction Character: '', '', 'significant', 'none'. bounce_any Logical. TRUE bounce pattern detected. bounce_above Integer. Number upward changes meeting threshold. bounce_below Integer. Number downward changes meeting threshold. reversals Integer. Detected reversals 0 non-0. returns Integer. Detected returns non-0 0. detailed = TRUE, returns additional columns: delta_down Logical. Significant downward trend. delta_up Logical. Significant upward trend. delta_none Logical. significant trend. bounce_up Logical. Significant bounce downward trend. bounce_down Logical. Significant bounce upward trend. bounce_none Logical. Significant bounces -trend data.

title: Check for Unsystematic Patterns in Cross-Price Data — check_unsystematic_cp

title: Extract Coefficients from a beezdemand_nlme Model — coef.beezdemand_nlme
text: object beezdemand_nlme object. type Character, type coefficients extract. One : "fixed": Returns fixed effects (equivalent fixef(object)). "random": Returns random effects (equivalent ranef(object)). "combined" (default): Returns subject-specific coefficients, subject's coefficient sum corresponding fixed effect subject's random effect deviation. equivalent stats::coef() nlme object returns. report_space Character. One "internal" (default), "natural", "log10". ... Additional arguments passed underlying nlme coefficient extraction functions (nlme::fixef(), nlme::ranef(), stats::coef.nlme()).

title: Extract Coefficients from a beezdemand_nlme Model — coef.beezdemand_nlme
text: Depending type: type="fixed": named numeric vector fixed-effect coefficients. type="random": data frame (list data frames multiple levels grouping) random effects, returned ranef.nlme(). type="combined": data frame rows subjects (id_var) columns Q0 alpha parameters, representing subject-specific estimates (log10 scale).

title: Extract Coefficients from a beezdemand_nlme Model — coef.beezdemand_nlme
code: # \donttest{ data(ko) fit <- fit_demand_mixed(ko, y_var = "y_ll4", x_var = "x",                         id_var = "monkey", equation_form = "zben") #> Generating starting values using method: 'heuristic' #> Using heuristic method for starting values. #> --- Fitting NLME Model --- #> Equation Form: zben #> Param Space: log10 #> NLME Formula: y_ll4 ~ Q0 * exp(-(10^alpha/Q0) * (10^Q0) * x) #> Start values (first few): Q0_int=2.27, alpha_int=-3 #> Number of fixed parameters: 2 (Q0: 1, alpha: 1) coef(fit, type = "fixed") #>        Q0     alpha  #>  2.158507 -4.586304  coef(fit, type = "random") #>              Q0         alpha #> A -8.202221e-12 -1.492133e-09 #> B -1.085836e-10 -2.435445e-11 #> C  1.167858e-10  1.516488e-09 coef(fit, type = "combined") #>         Q0     alpha #> A 2.158507 -4.586304 #> B 2.158507 -4.586304 #> C 2.158507 -4.586304 # }

what: Compare Nested Hurdle Demand Models — compare_hurdle_models
title: Compare Nested Hurdle Demand Models — compare_hurdle_models
text: Performs likelihood ratio test comparing two nested hurdle demand models. Typically used test whether adding random effect alpha (c_i) significantly improves model fit (3-RE vs 2-RE models).

title: Compare Nested Hurdle Demand Models — compare_hurdle_models
code: compare_hurdle_models(model_full, model_reduced)

title: Compare Nested Hurdle Demand Models — compare_hurdle_models
text: model_full beezdemand_hurdle object 3 random effects. model_reduced beezdemand_hurdle object 2 random effects.

title: Compare Nested Hurdle Demand Models — compare_hurdle_models
text: Invisibly returns list : lr_stat Likelihood ratio test statistic df Degrees freedom p_value P-value chi-squared distribution model_comparison Data frame model comparison statistics

title: Compare Nested Hurdle Demand Models — compare_hurdle_models
code: # \donttest{ data(apt) fit3 <- fit_demand_hurdle(apt, y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id",                           random_effects = c("zeros", "q0", "alpha")) #> Sample size may be too small for reliable estimation. #>   Subjects: 10, Parameters: 12, Recommended minimum: 60 subjects. #>   Consider using more subjects or the simpler 2-RE model. #> Fitting HurdleDemand3RE model... #>   Part II: zhao_exponential #>   Subjects: 10, Observations: 160 #>   Fixed parameters: 12, Random effects per subject: 3 #>   Optimizing... #>   Converged in 81 iterations #>   Computing standard errors... #> Done. Log-likelihood: 32.81 fit2 <- fit_demand_hurdle(apt, y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id",                           random_effects = c("zeros", "q0")) #> Sample size may be too small for reliable estimation. #>   Subjects: 10, Parameters: 9, Recommended minimum: 45 subjects. #>   Consider using more subjects or the simpler 2-RE model. #> Fitting HurdleDemand2RE model... #>   Part II: zhao_exponential #>   Subjects: 10, Observations: 160 #>   Fixed parameters: 9, Random effects per subject: 2 #>   Optimizing... #>   Converged in 93 iterations #>   Computing standard errors... #> Done. Log-likelihood: 2.31 compare_hurdle_models(fit3, fit2) #>  #> Likelihood Ratio Test #> ===================== #>           Model n_RE   LogLik df       AIC       BIC #>     Full (3 RE)    3 32.81453 12 -41.62905 -4.726965 #>  Reduced (2 RE)    2  2.30934  9  13.38132 41.057884 #>  #> LR statistic: 61.0104  #> df: 3  #> p-value: 3.5757e-13  # }

title: Compare Demand Models — compare_models
text: Compare multiple demand models using information criteria likelihood ratio tests (applicable). Works beezdemand model classes.

title: Compare Demand Models — compare_models
code: compare_models(..., test = c("auto", "lrt", "none"))

title: Compare Demand Models — compare_models
text: ... Two model objects class beezdemand_hurdle, beezdemand_nlme, beezdemand_fixed. test Character; type statistical test. One : "auto" (default): Use LRT models nested comparable, otherwise IC-. "lrt": Force likelihood ratio test (requires nested models backend). "none": report information criteria, p-value.

title: Compare Demand Models — compare_models
text: object class beezdemand_model_comparison containing: comparison Data frame model fit statistics test_type Type test performed lrt_results LRT results performed (NULL otherwise) best_model Index best model BIC notes Character vector notes/warnings nesting_verified Logical; always FALSE since nesting automatically verified. Users must ensure models properly nested valid LRT interpretation.

title: Compare Demand Models — compare_models
text: Models compared using AIC BIC. models statistical backend (e.g., two hurdle models two NLME models), likelihood ratio tests can performed models nested. comparing models different backends (e.g., hurdle vs NLME), information criteria comparisons possible since likelihoods directly comparable LRT purposes.

title: Compare Demand Models — compare_models
text: likelihood ratio test (LRT) assumes : models nested (reduced model special case full model obtained constraining parameters). models fit identical data. null hypothesis, LR statistic follows chi-square distribution degrees freedom equal difference number parameters. Important caveat mixed-effects models: variance components tested boundary (e.g., testing whether random effect variance zero), standard chi-square distribution appropriate. correct null distribution mixture chi-squares (Stram & Lee, 1994). p-values reported use standard chi-square approximation, conservative (p-values large) boundary tests. function automatically verify models nested. Users ensure models properly nested interpreting LRT p-values.

title: Compare Demand Models — compare_models
text: Stram, D. O., & Lee, J. W. (1994). Variance components testing longitudinal mixed effects model. Biometrics, 50(4), 1171-1177.

title: Compare Demand Models — compare_models
code: # \donttest{ data(apt) fit2 <- fit_demand_hurdle(apt, y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id",                           random_effects = c("zeros", "q0")) #> Sample size may be too small for reliable estimation. #>   Subjects: 10, Parameters: 9, Recommended minimum: 45 subjects. #>   Consider using more subjects or the simpler 2-RE model. #> Fitting HurdleDemand2RE model... #>   Part II: zhao_exponential #>   Subjects: 10, Observations: 160 #>   Fixed parameters: 9, Random effects per subject: 2 #>   Optimizing... #>   Converged in 93 iterations #>   Computing standard errors... #> Done. Log-likelihood: 2.31 fit3 <- fit_demand_hurdle(apt, y_var = "y", x_var = "x", id_var = "id",                           random_effects = c("zeros", "q0", "alpha")) #> Sample size may be too small for reliable estimation. #>   Subjects: 10, Parameters: 12, Recommended minimum: 60 subjects. #>   Consider using more subjects or the simpler 2-RE model. #> Fitting HurdleDemand3RE model... #>   Part II: zhao_exponential #>   Subjects: 10, Observations: 160 #>   Fixed parameters: 12, Random effects per subject: 3 #>   Optimizing... #>   Converged in 81 iterations #>   Computing standard errors... #> Done. Log-likelihood: 32.81 compare_models(fit2, fit3) #> Note: LRT assumes models are nested (reduced model is a special case of the full model). Nesting is not automatically verified. See ?compare_models for guidance on valid model comparisons. #>  #> Model Comparison #> ==================================================  #>  #>    Model             Class    Backend nobs df  logLik      AIC     BIC #>  Model_1 beezdemand_hurdle TMB_hurdle  160  9  2.3093  13.3813 41.0579 #>  Model_2 beezdemand_hurdle TMB_hurdle  160 12 32.8145 -41.6291 -4.7270 #>  delta_AIC delta_BIC #>    55.0104   45.7848 #>     0.0000    0.0000 #>  #> Best model by BIC: Model_2  #>  #> Likelihood Ratio Tests: #> ----------------------------------------  #>          Comparison LR_stat df  p_value #>  Model_1 vs Model_2 61.0104  3 3.58e-13 #>  #> Notes: #>   - LRT nesting assumption not verified.  # }

what: Compute Normalized Alpha (Alpha Star) via the Delta Method — .calc_alpha_star
title: Compute Normalized Alpha (Alpha Star) via the Delta Method — .calc_alpha_star
text: Implements Strategy B normalization demand elasticity parameter \(\alpha\) values comparable across different \(k\) values (Rzeszutek et al., 2025). formula \(\alpha^* = -\alpha / \ln(1 - 1/(k \cdot \ln(b)))\) \(b\) logarithmic base used demand equation (10 HS/Koff, \(e\) hurdle models).

title: Compute Normalized Alpha (Alpha Star) via the Delta Method — .calc_alpha_star
code: .calc_alpha_star(params, param_scales, vcov = NULL, base = c("e", "10"))

title: Compute Normalized Alpha (Alpha Star) via the Delta Method — .calc_alpha_star
text: params Named list parameter values. Must contain entries matchable alpha k (e.g., alpha, log_alpha, log10_alpha). param_scales Named list indicating scale parameter params: "natural", "log", "log10". vcov Optional. Either variance–covariance matrix named rows/columns, named numeric vector standard errors alpha k parameters. base Character; logarithmic base: "e" (natural log, used hurdle models) "10" (log10, used HS/Koff equations).

title: Compute Normalized Alpha (Alpha Star) via the Delta Method — .calc_alpha_star
text: list elements: estimate Numeric scalar; alpha_star value, NA. se Numeric scalar; delta-method SE, NA. note Character NULL; diagnostic message alpha_star computed.

title: Compute Normalized Alpha (Alpha Star) via the Delta Method — .calc_alpha_star
text: Rzeszutek, M. J., Regnier, S. D., Franck, C. T., & Koffarnus, M. N. (2025). Overviewing exponential model demand introducing simplification solves issues span, scale, zeros. Experimental Clinical Psychopharmacology.

what: Simulate within-subject demand data for TICKET-011 Phase 2 parity tests — .simulate_within_subject_demand
title: Simulate within-subject demand data for TICKET-011 Phase 2 parity tests — .simulate_within_subject_demand
text: Generates long-format demand data subject observed every price every level -subject condition factor. Used Phase 2 parity tests confirm fit_demand_tmb() matches fit_demand_mixed() factor-expanded random-effects specifications (pdDiag(Q0+alpha~condition), pdSymm(Q0+alpha~condition) etc.).

title: Simulate within-subject demand data for TICKET-011 Phase 2 parity tests — .simulate_within_subject_demand
code: .simulate_within_subject_demand(   n_subjects = 30,   n_conditions = 3,   prices = c(0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20),   log_q0_pop = log(20),   log_alpha_pop = log(0.005),   delta_q0 = NULL,   delta_alpha = NULL,   sigma_b = 0.3,   sigma_d = 0.3,   rho_bd = 0,   sigma_e = 0.1,   seed = NULL )

title: Simulate within-subject demand data for TICKET-011 Phase 2 parity tests — .simulate_within_subject_demand
text: n_subjects Integer; number subjects. n_conditions Integer; number within-subject condition levels (named "C1", "C2", ...). prices Numeric vector prices subject sees every condition. log_q0_pop Numeric; population log-Q0. log_alpha_pop Numeric; population log-alpha. delta_q0 Numeric vector length n_conditions; per-condition shifts log-Q0. Defaults 0 conditions. delta_alpha Numeric vector length n_conditions; per-condition shifts log-alpha. Defaults 0 conditions. sigma_b Numeric; SD per-(subject, condition) Q0 random deviation. sigma_d Numeric; SD per-(subject, condition) alpha random deviation. rho_bd Numeric; correlation b d within (subject, condition). sigma_e Numeric; residual SD log-y. seed Optional integer seed reproducibility.

title: Simulate within-subject demand data for TICKET-011 Phase 2 parity tests — .simulate_within_subject_demand
text: tibble columns id (factor), condition (factor), x (price), y (consumption). Long-format, one row per (subject, condition, price).

title: Simulate within-subject demand data for TICKET-011 Phase 2 parity tests — .simulate_within_subject_demand
text: Data-generating process: subject condition c price p consumption $$y_{,c,p} = Q_{0,,c} \cdot \exp(-\alpha_{,c} \cdot Q_{0,,c} \cdot p) \cdot \exp(\epsilon)$$ $$\log Q_{0,,c} = \log Q_{0,\text{pop}} + \delta^{Q_0}_c + b_{,c}$$ $$\log \alpha_{,c} = \log \alpha_{\text{pop}} + \delta^{\alpha}_c + d_{,c}$$ per-condition shifts delta_q0[c], delta_alpha[c] per-subject per-condition random deviations (b_{,c}, d_{,c}) ~ N(0, Sigma).

what: Fit cross-price demand with NLS (+ robust fallbacks) — fit_cp_nls
title: Fit cross-price demand with NLS (+ robust fallbacks) — fit_cp_nls
text: Fits cross-price demand curve using log10-parameterization numerical stability. optimizer estimates parameters log10 scale applicable, ensuring positive constraints naturally satisfied. Equation forms: Exponentiated (default): $$y = Q_{alone} \cdot 10^{\cdot \exp(-\beta \cdot x)}$$ Exponential (fits log10 response scale): $$\log_{10}(y) = \log_{10}(Q_{alone}) + \cdot \exp(-\beta \cdot x)$$ Additive (level \(y\)): $$y = Q_{alone} + \cdot \exp(-\beta \cdot x)$$ \(x\) alternative product price ("cross" price) \(y\) consumption target good. Optimizer parameters (log10 parameterization): log10_qalone: \(\log_{10}(Q_{alone})\) - baseline consumption alternative effectively absent. : cross-price interaction intensity; sign magnitude reflect substitution/complementarity. Unconstrained (can negative substitutes). log10_beta: \(\log_{10}(\beta)\) - rate cross-price influence decays \(x\) increases. Natural-scale values recovered \(Q_{alone} = 10^{log10\_qalone}\) \(\beta = 10^{log10\_beta}\). function first attempts multi-start nonlinear least squares fit (nls.multstart). fails—explicit start_values provided—falls back minpack.lm::nlsLM. Optionally, make final attempt nlsr::wrapnlsr. Returns either fitted model structured object metadata downstream methods.

title: Fit cross-price demand with NLS (+ robust fallbacks) — fit_cp_nls
code: fit_cp_nls(   data,   equation = c("exponentiated", "exponential", "additive"),   x_var = "x",   y_var = "y",   start_values = NULL,   start_vals = lifecycle::deprecated(),   iter = 100,   bounds = NULL,   fallback_to_nlsr = TRUE,   return_all = TRUE )

title: Fit cross-price demand with NLS (+ robust fallbacks) — fit_cp_nls
text: data data frame columns price consumption. Additional columns ignored. Input validated internally. equation Character string; model family, one c("exponentiated", "exponential", "additive"). Default "exponentiated". x_var Character string; name price column data. Default "x". column renamed "x" internally; see validate_cp_data(). y_var Character string; name consumption column data. Default "y". column renamed "y" internally. start_values Optional named list initial values parameters log10_qalone, , log10_beta. NULL, function derives plausible ranges data uses multi-start search. start_vals Use start_values instead. iter Integer; number random starts nls.multstart (default 100). bounds Deprecated. Log10-parameterized parameters naturally unbounded. argument ignored retained backwards compatibility. fallback_to_nlsr Logical; TRUE (default), try nlsr::wrapnlsr multi-start NLS nlsLM fail. return_all Logical; TRUE (default), return list containing model useful metadata. FALSE, return bare fitted model object.

title: Fit cross-price demand with NLS (+ robust fallbacks) — fit_cp_nls
text: return_all = TRUE (default): list class "cp_model_nls": model: fitted object successful backend. method: one "nls_multstart", "nlsLM", "wrapnlsr". equation: model family used. start_vals: named list starting values (final used; kept backward compatibility). nlsLM_fit, nlsr_fit: fits later stages (attempted). data: 2-column data frame actually fit. return_all = FALSE: fitted model object successful backend.

title: Fit cross-price demand with NLS (+ robust fallbacks) — fit_cp_nls
text: Start values. start_values missing, function: (1) estimates reasonable range log10_qalone observed y, (2) estimates log10_beta price range, (3) launches multi-start grid nls.multstart. Zero handling exponential equation. Since exponential equation fits \(\log_{10}(y)\) scale, observations \(y \le 0\) automatically removed warning. Use exponentiated additive forms need retain zero consumption values. Fitting pipeline (short-circuiting): nls.multstart::nls_multstart() random starts. fails (start_values provided): minpack.lm::nlsLM() using start_values (user internally estimated). fails fallback_to_nlsr = TRUE: nlsr::wrapnlsr(). returned object class "cp_model_nls" (return_all = TRUE) components: model, method (algorithm used), equation, start_vals, nlsLM_fit, nlsr_fit, data used. convenient custom print/summary/plot methods.

title: Fit cross-price demand with NLS (+ robust fallbacks) — fit_cp_nls
text: Check convergence codes residual diagnostics underlying fit. Poor scaling extreme y dispersion can make parameters weakly identified. "exponential", model fits \(\log_{10}(y)\) scale internally.

title: Fit cross-price demand with NLS (+ robust fallbacks) — fit_cp_nls
code: ## --- Example: Real data (E-Cigarettes, id = 1) --- dat <- structure(list(   id = c(1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1),   x = c(2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64),   y = c(3, 5, 5, 16, 17, 13),   target = c("alt", "alt", "alt", "alt", "alt", "alt"),   group = c("E-Cigarettes", "E-Cigarettes", "E-Cigarettes",             "E-Cigarettes", "E-Cigarettes", "E-Cigarettes") ), row.names = c(NA, -6L), class = c("tbl_df", "tbl", "data.frame"))  ## Fit the default (exponentiated) cross-price form fit_ecig <- fit_cp_nls(dat, equation = "exponentiated", return_all = TRUE) summary(fit_ecig)         # model summary #> Cross-Price Demand Model Summary #> ================================ #>  #> Model Specification: #> Equation type: exponentiated  #> Functional form: y ~ (10^log10_qalone) * 10^(I * exp(-(10^log10_beta) * x))  #> Fitting method: nls_multstart  #> Method details: Multiple starting values optimization with nls.multstart  #>  #> Coefficients: #>               Estimate Std. Error t value  Pr(>|t|)     #> log10_qalone  1.194135   0.060311 19.7995 0.0002815 *** #> I            -1.268376   0.837302 -1.5148 0.2270524     #> log10_beta   -0.737728   0.265129 -2.7825 0.0688459 .   #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #> Confidence Intervals: #>               2.5 % 97.5 % #> log10_qalone  1.002  1.386 #> I            -3.933  1.396 #> log10_beta   -1.581  0.106 #>  #> Fit Statistics: #> R-squared: 0.8597  #> AIC: 34.06  #> BIC: 33.23  #>  #> Parameter Interpretation (natural scale): #> qalone (Q_alone): 15.64  - consumption at zero alternative price #> I: -1.268  - interaction parameter (substitution direction) #> beta: 0.1829  - sensitivity parameter (sensitivity of relation to price) #>  #> Optimizer parameters (log10 scale): #> log10_qalone: 1.194  #> log10_beta: -0.7377  fit_ecig$method           # backend actually used (e.g., "nls_multstart") #> [1] "nls_multstart" coef(fit_ecig$model)      # parameter estimates: log10_qalone, I, log10_beta #> log10_qalone            I   log10_beta  #>    1.1941354   -1.2683758   -0.7377282

Default "hs". k Scaling constant. Numeric value (fixed), "ind" (individual), "fit" (free parameter), "range" (data-driven). Default 2. agg Character. Aggregation method: "Mean", "Pooled", NULL individual fits. Default NULL. x_var Character. Name price column. Default "x". y_var Character. Name consumption column. Default "y". id_var Character. Name subject identifier column. Default "id". param_space Character. Parameterization used fitting. One : "natural": fit Q0, alpha (k k = "fit") natural scale "log10": fit log10(Q0), log10(alpha) (log10(k) k = "fit") Optional character vector column names group . supplied, fits run separately within unique combination columns. what: Fit Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — fit_demand_hurdle
title: Fit Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — fit_demand_hurdle
text: Fits two-part hurdle model demand data using TMB (Template Model Builder). Part models probability zero consumption using logistic regression. Part II models log-consumption given positive response using nonlinear mixed effects model.

title: Fit Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — fit_demand_hurdle
code: fit_demand_hurdle(   data,   y_var,   x_var,   id_var,   random_effects = c("zeros", "q0", "alpha"),   epsilon = 0.001,   start_values = NULL,   tmb_control = list(max_iter = 200, eval_max = 1000, trace = 0),   verbose = 1,   part2 = c("zhao_exponential", "exponential", "simplified_exponential"),   ... )

title: Fit Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — fit_demand_hurdle
text: data data frame containing demand data. y_var Character string specifying column name consumption values. x_var Character string specifying column name price. id_var Character string specifying column name subject IDs. random_effects Character vector specifying random effects include. Options "zeros" (a_i Part ), "q0" (b_i intensity), "alpha" (c_i elasticity). Default c("zeros", "q0", "alpha") full 3-random-effect model. Use c("zeros", "q0") simplified 2-random-effect model (fixed alpha across subjects). epsilon Small constant added price log transformation Part . Used handle zero prices: log(price + epsilon). Default 0.001. start_values Optional named list starting values optimization. NULL (default), sensible defaults used. tmb_control List control parameters TMB optimization: max_iter Maximum number optimization iterations (default 200) eval_max Maximum number function evaluations (default 1000) trace Print optimization trace: 0 = none, 1 = (default 0) verbose Integer controlling output verbosity: 0 = silent, 1 = progress messages, 2 = detailed optimization trace. Default 1. part2 Character string selecting Part II mean function. Options "zhao_exponential" (default; Q0 normalization exponent), "exponential" (HS-standardized; Q0 inside exponent), "simplified_exponential" (SND/log-linear; k parameter). ... Additional arguments (reserved future use).

title: Fit Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — fit_demand_hurdle
text: object class beezdemand_hurdle containing: model List coefficients, se, variance_components, correlations random_effects Matrix empirical Bayes random effect estimates subject_pars Data frame subject-specific parameters including Q0, alpha, breakpoint, Pmax, Omax tmb_obj TMB objective function object opt Optimization result nlminb sdr TMB sdreport object call matched call data Original data used fitting param_info List y_var, x_var, id_var, n_subjects, n_obs, etc. converged Logical indicating convergence loglik Log-likelihood convergence AIC, BIC Information criteria error_message Error message fitting failed, NULL otherwise

title: Fit Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — fit_demand_hurdle
text: model structure : Part (Binary - probability zero consumption): $$logit(\pi_{ij}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \log(price + \epsilon) + a_i$$ Part II (Continuous - log consumption given positive): 3 random effects (random_effects = c("zeros", "q0", "alpha")): $$\log(Q_{ij}) = (\log Q_0 + b_i) + k \cdot (\exp(-\alpha_i \cdot price) - 1) + \epsilon_{ij}$$ \(\alpha_i = \exp(\log(\alpha) + c_i)\) \(k = \exp(\log(k))\). 2 random effects (random_effects = c("zeros", "q0")): $$\log(Q_{ij}) = (\log Q_0 + b_i) + k \cdot (\exp(-\alpha \cdot price) - 1) + \epsilon_{ij}$$ \(\alpha = \exp(\log(\alpha))\) \(k = \exp(\log(k))\). Random effects follow multivariate normal distribution unstructured covariance matrix. Use compare_hurdle_models likelihood ratio tests comparing nested models.

title: Fit Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — fit_demand_hurdle
text: TMB backend estimates positive-constrained parameters natural-log scale: \(\log(Q_0)\), \(\log(\alpha)\), \(\log(k)\). Reporting methods (summary(), tidy(), coef()) can back-transform natural scale present parameters \(\log_{10}\) scale. compare \(\alpha\) estimates models fit \(\log_{10}\) space, use: $$\log_{10}(\alpha) = \log(\alpha) / \log(10).$$

title: Fit Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — fit_demand_hurdle

what: Fit Nonlinear Mixed-Effects Demand Model — fit_demand_mixed
title: Fit Nonlinear Mixed-Effects Demand Model — fit_demand_mixed
text: Fits nonlinear mixed-effects model behavioral economic demand data. function allows Q0 alpha parameters vary specified factors supports different demand equation forms.

title: Fit Nonlinear Mixed-Effects Demand Model — fit_demand_mixed
code: fit_demand_mixed(   data,   y_var,   x_var,   id_var,   factors = NULL,   factor_interaction = FALSE,   equation_form = c("zben", "simplified", "exponentiated"),   param_space = c("log10", "natural"),   k = NULL,   custom_model_formula = NULL,   fixed_rhs = NULL,   continuous_covariates = NULL,   start_value_method = c("heuristic", "pooled_nls"),   random_effects = Q0 + alpha ~ 1,   covariance_structure = c("pdDiag", "pdSymm"),   start_values = NULL,   collapse_levels = NULL,   nlme_control = list(msMaxIter = 200, niterEM = 100, maxIter = 200, pnlsTol = 0.001,     tolerance = 1e-06, apVar = TRUE, minScale = 1e-09, opt = "nlminb", msVerbose = FALSE),   method = "ML",   ... )

title: Fit Nonlinear Mixed-Effects Demand Model — fit_demand_mixed
text: data data frame. y_var Character string, name dependent variable column. equation_form = "zben", log-transformed consumption (e.g., "y_ll4"). equation_form = "simplified" "exponentiated", raw, untransformed consumption (e.g., "y"). x_var Character string, name independent variable column (e.g., "x", price). id_var Character string, name subject/group identifier column random effects. factors character vector factor names (two) Q0 alpha expected vary (e.g., c("dose", "treatment")). factor_interaction Logical. TRUE two factors provided, interaction term included fixed effects Q0 alpha. Defaults FALSE (additive effects). equation_form Character string specifying demand equation form. Options : "zben" (default): Assumes y_var log-transformed. Equation: y_var ~ Q0 * exp(-(10^alpha / Q0) * (10^Q0) * x_var). Model parameter Q0 represents log10(True Max Consumption). Model parameter alpha represents log10(True Alpha Sensitivity). "simplified": Assumes y_var raw (untransformed) consumption. Equation: y_var ~ (10^Q0) * exp(-(10^alpha) * (10^Q0) * x_var). Model parameter Q0 represents log10(True Max Consumption). Model parameter alpha represents log10(True Alpha Sensitivity). "exponentiated": Koffarnus et al. (2015) exponentiated equation. Assumes y_var raw (untransformed) consumption. Requires k parameter. Equation (log10 param_space): y_var ~ (10^Q0) * 10^(k * (exp(-(10^alpha) * (10^Q0) * x_var) - 1)). Equation (natural param_space): y_var ~ Q0 * 10^(k * (exp(-alpha * Q0 * x_var) - 1)). param_space Character. Parameterization used fitting core demand parameters. One : "log10": treat Q0 alpha log10(Q0) log10(alpha) (default) "natural": treat Q0 alpha natural-scale parameters Notes: equation_form = "zben", "log10" currently supported. equation_form = "simplified" "exponentiated", "log10" "natural" supported. k Numeric. Range parameter (log10 units) used equation_form = "exponentiated". NULL (default), k calculated data range: log10(max(y)) - log10(min(y)) + 0.5. Ignored equation forms. custom_model_formula optional custom nonlinear model formula (nlme format). provided, overrides equation_form. user responsible ensuring y_var scale matches formula starting values appropriate. formula use parameters named Q0 alpha. fixed_rhs Optional one-sided formula character string specifying right-hand side (RHS) fixed-effects linear models Q0 alpha. provided, RHS used parameters overrides factors, factor_interaction, continuous_covariates building fixed-effects design matrix. Example: "~ 1 + drug * dose + session". continuous_covariates Optional character vector continuous (numeric) predictor names included additively fixed-effects RHS fixed_rhs NULL. variables coerced factors stored downstream functions (e.g., plotting) condition . start_value_method Character, method generate starting values start_values NULL. Options: "heuristic" (default, uses data-driven heuristics) "pooled_nls" (fits simpler pooled NLS model first; falls back heuristic NLS fails). random_effects formula list formulas random effects structure. Default nlme::pdDiag(Q0 + alpha ~ 1). covariance_structure Character, covariance structure random effects. Options: "pdDiag" (default) "pdSymm" start_values Optional named list starting values fixed effects. NULL, defaults estimated based equation_form y_var scale. collapse_levels Optional named list specifying factor level collapsing separately Q0 alpha parameters. Structure:   Either Q0 alpha () can omitted use original factor levels parameter. allows different collapsing schemes parameter. Ignored fixed_rhs provided. nlme_control Control parameters nlme::nlme(). method Fitting method nlme::nlme() ("ML" "REML"). Default "ML". ... Additional arguments passed nlme::nlme().

what: Fit Mixed-Effects Demand Models via TMB — fit_demand_tmb
title: Fit Mixed-Effects Demand Models via TMB — fit_demand_tmb
text: Fits nonlinear mixed-effects demand models using Template Model Builder (TMB) exact automatic differentiation Laplace approximation. provides reliable convergence traditional NLME approaches fail.

title: Fit Mixed-Effects Demand Models via TMB — fit_demand_tmb
code: fit_demand_tmb(   data,   y_var = "y",   x_var = "x",   id_var = "id",   equation = c("exponentiated", "exponential", "simplified", "zben"),   estimate_k = TRUE,   k = NULL,   random_effects = Q0 + alpha ~ 1,   covariance_structure = c("pdSymm", "pdDiag"),   factors = NULL,   factor_interaction = FALSE,   continuous_covariates = NULL,   collapse_levels = NULL,   start_values = NULL,   tmb_control = list(iter_max = 1000, eval_max = 2000),   multi_start = TRUE,   validate_subject_pars = TRUE,   verbose = 1,   ... )

title: Fit Mixed-Effects Demand Models via TMB — fit_demand_tmb
text: data data frame long format columns subject ID, price, consumption. y_var Character. Name consumption/response variable. x_var Character. Name price variable. id_var Character. Name subject identifier variable. equation Character. demand equation fit. One : "exponentiated" Koffarnus et al. (2015). Gaussian raw Q. Zeros allowed. k parameter. "exponential" Hursh & Silberberg (2008). Gaussian log(Q). Observations Q = 0 automatically dropped. k parameter. "simplified" Simplified exponential (k). Gaussian raw Q. Zeros allowed. "zben" Zero-bounded exponential (k). Gaussian LL4- transformed Q. User must pass LL4-transformed y_var. Note: Q0 log10 scale clamped minimum 0.001 avoid singularity Q0 = 1 (log10(Q0) = 0 causes division zero decay rate). Subjects estimated Q0 near 1 may biased parameter estimates. estimate_k Logical. TRUE (default), estimate k free parameter. FALSE, fix k value given k. relevant "exponentiated" "exponential" equations. k Numeric NULL. Fixed value k estimate_k = FALSE. NULL estimate_k = FALSE, k defaults 2. random_effects Specification subject-level random effects. Accepts following, order generality: formula (default) Q0 + alpha ~ 1 – random intercepts parameters (equivalent legacy c("q0", "alpha") shortcut). Q0 ~ 1 limits REs Q0. Formulas factor-expanded RHS (e.g., Q0 + alpha ~ condition Q0 + alpha ~ condition - 1) now supported Phase 2 – see TICKET-011 Phase 2 details. within-subject factor must vary within id; pure -subject factors belong factors, RE formula. nlme::pdMat e.g., nlme::pdDiag(Q0 + alpha ~ 1) nlme::pdSymm(Q0 + alpha ~ condition). Pre-constructed pdMat objects accepted covariance class honored (overrides covariance_structure). list pdMat / nlme::pdBlocked Multi-block covariance structures like list(pdSymm(Q0+alpha~1), pdDiag(Q0+alpha~cond-1)). Parsed, fitting deferred Phase 3 TICKET-011 – use fit_demand_mixed() multi-block fits meantime. character vector (deprecated) c("q0", "alpha") "q0". Soft-deprecated 0.4.0; emits lifecycle::deprecate_soft() message. Translated internally formula Q0 + alpha ~ 1 Q0 ~ 1. covariance_structure "pdSymm" (default; unstructured) "pdDiag" (diagonal). Applies random_effects formula; ignored pre-constructed pdMat / list / pdBlocked inputs. factors Character vector factor variable names group comparisons. factor_interaction Logical. TRUE two factors provided, include interaction. continuous_covariates Character vector continuous covariate names. collapse_levels Named list asymmetric factor collapsing. Structure: list(Q0 = list(factor = list(new = c(old))), alpha = list(...)). start_values Named list starting values. NULL, data-driven defaults used. tmb_control List control parameters optimizer: optimizer Character. "nlminb" (default) "L-BFGS-B". L-BFGS-B can recover nlminb convergence failures (code 1 8). iter_max Maximum iterations (default 1000). eval_max Maximum function evaluations (default 2000). applies nlminb; L-BFGS-B function evaluation limit. rel_tol Relative convergence tolerance (default 1e-10). applies nlminb. lower Named numeric vector lower bounds optimizer-scale parameters (default NULL = bounds). Names must match optimizer parameter names (e.g., log_k, beta_q0, logsigma_b). Note parameters log-space: e.g., constrain k 0.14 55, use lower = c(log_k = -2), upper = c(log_k = 4). bound name applies occurrences parameter (e.g., elements beta_q0). upper Named numeric vector upper bounds (see lower). warm_start Named numeric vector starting values optimizer space (e.g., previous fit$opt$par). provided, multi_start automatically disabled. differs start_values, operates parameter space TMB::MakeADFun(). Length must match number free parameters. trace Non-negative integer controlling optimizer trace output (default 0). explicitly set, inherits verbose >= 2. multi_start Logical. TRUE (default), try 3 starting value sets select best. validate_subject_pars Logical. TRUE (default), validate every column fixed-effect design matrices constant within id computing subject_pars. factor continuous covariate varies within subject, Q0/alpha/Pmax/Omax set NA_real_ affected subjects warning names offending columns. Set FALSE force row-order-dependent values (recommended; proper factor-expanded RE support lands TICKET-011 Phases 2-3). verbose Integer. Verbosity level: 0 = silent, 1 = progress, 2 = debug. ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: Fit Mixed-Effects Demand Models via TMB — fit_demand_tmb
text: object class beezdemand_tmb containing: model List coefficients, se, variance_components subject_pars Data frame subject-specific Q0, alpha, Pmax, Omax tmb_obj TMB objective function object opt Optimization result (normalized across optimizers) sdr TMB sdreport object converged Logical convergence indicator loglik Log-likelihood convergence AIC Akaike Information Criterion BIC Bayesian Information Criterion data Original data (filtering) param_info List model metadata formula_details Design matrix formula information collapse_info Collapse levels information (used)

title: Fit Mixed-Effects Demand Models via TMB — fit_demand_tmb
text: Traditional NLME approaches using nlme::nlme() universally fail demand equations PNLS algorithm numerical finite-difference gradients navigate likelihood surface. TMB succeeds using exact automatic differentiation, Laplace approximation, joint marginal likelihood optimization. estimate_k = TRUE, k estimated free parameter alongside Q0 alpha. typically improves model fit substantially. conventional fixed-k approach (Hursh & Silberberg, 2008) often overestimates k 3-8x. Error model considerations: exponentiated simplified equations use Gaussian error model raw consumption (Q), assigns non-zero density negative values. data many near-zero observations, prefer exponential (Gaussian log Q, zeros dropped) zben (Gaussian LL4-transformed Q, zeros handled transformation).

what: Get Pairwise Comparisons for Demand Parameters — get_demand_comparisons
title: Get Pairwise Comparisons for Demand Parameters — get_demand_comparisons
text: Conducts pairwise comparisons Q0 /alpha parameters beezdemand_nlme model across levels specified factors. Comparisons performed log10 scale parameters. Results include estimates differences (log10 scale) optionally, ratios (natural scale applying 10^difference).

title: Get Pairwise Comparisons for Demand Parameters — get_demand_comparisons
code: get_demand_comparisons(fit_obj, ...)  # Default S3 method get_demand_comparisons(fit_obj, ...)  # S3 method for class 'beezdemand_nlme' get_demand_comparisons(   fit_obj,   params_to_compare = c("Q0", "alpha"),   compare_specs = NULL,   contrast_type = "pairwise",   contrast_by = NULL,   adjust = "tukey",   at = NULL,   ci_level = 0.95,   report_ratios = TRUE,   ... )

title: Get Pairwise Comparisons for Demand Parameters — get_demand_comparisons
text: fit_obj beezdemand_nlme object. ... Additional arguments passed emmeans::emmeans() emmeans::contrast(). params_to_compare Character vector: "Q0", "alpha", c("Q0", "alpha"). Default c("Q0", "alpha"). compare_specs formula specifying factors whose levels included EMM calculation prior contrasting. defines "cells" design EMMs. E.g., ~ factor1 (EMMs levels factor1, averaging others), ~ factor1 * factor2 (EMMs cells factor1 x factor2). NULL, defaults interaction factors fit_obj$param_info$factors. contrast_type Character string specifying type contrast (passed method emmeans::contrast). Commonly "pairwise", "revpairwise", "eff", "consec", "poly". Default "pairwise". contrast_by Optional character vector factor names condition contrasts (passed emmeans::contrast). NULL (default), contrasts performed primary terms implied compare_specs contrast_type. Example: compare_specs = ~ dose * drug, contrast_type = "pairwise", contrast_by = "dose", perform pairwise comparisons drug levels within level dose. Note: original fit_obj model additive factors involved (.e., interaction term fitted), specifying contrast_by result identical contrast estimates across levels contrast_by variable(s). cases, consider analyzing main effects directly (e.g., compare_specs = ~drug, contrast_by = NULL). adjust P-value adjustment method. Default "tukey". Optional named list emmeans::ref_grid(). ci_level Confidence level. Default 0.95. report_ratios Logical. TRUE, reports contrasts ratios. Default TRUE.

title: Get Pairwise Comparisons for Demand Parameters — get_demand_comparisons
text: list named parameter. element contains: emmeans Tibble EMMs (log10 scale) CIs. contrasts_log10 Tibble comparisons (log10 differences) CIs p-values. contrasts_ratio (report_ratios=TRUE successful) Tibble comparisons ratios (natural scale), CIs ratios. S3 class beezdemand_comparison assigned.

title: Get Pairwise Comparisons for Demand Parameters — get_demand_comparisons
code: # \donttest{ data(ko, package = "beezdemand") ko$y_ll4 <- ll4(ko$y, lambda = 4) fit <- fit_demand_mixed(ko, y_var = "y_ll4", x_var = "x",   id_var = "monkey", factors = "dose", equation_form = "zben") #> Generating starting values using method: 'heuristic' #> Using heuristic method for starting values. #> --- Fitting NLME Model --- #> Equation Form: zben #> Param Space: log10 #> NLME Formula: y_ll4 ~ Q0 * exp(-(10^alpha/Q0) * (10^Q0) * x) #> Start values (first few): Q0_int=2.27, alpha_int=-3 #> Number of fixed parameters: 10 (Q0: 5, alpha: 5) get_demand_comparisons(fit) #> Using default 'compare_specs': ~ dose for EMMs. #>  #> --- Processing comparisons for parameter: Q0 --- #> Note: adjust = "tukey" was changed to "sidak" #> because "tukey" is only appropriate for one set of pairwise comparisons #> Note: adjust = "tukey" was changed to "sidak" #> because "tukey" is only appropriate for one set of pairwise comparisons #>  #> --- Processing comparisons for parameter: alpha --- #> Note: adjust = "tukey" was changed to "sidak" #> because "tukey" is only appropriate for one set of pairwise comparisons #> Note: adjust = "tukey" was changed to "sidak" #> because "tukey" is only appropriate for one set of pairwise comparisons #> Demand Parameter Comparisons (from beezdemand_nlme fit) #> EMMs computed over: ~dose  #> Contrast type: pairwise #> P-value adjustment method: tukey  #> ==================================================  #>  # }

what: Get Estimated Marginal Means for Demand Parameters — get_demand_param_emms
title: Get Estimated Marginal Means for Demand Parameters — get_demand_param_emms
text: Calculates Estimated Marginal Means (EMMs) Q0 alpha parameters beezdemand_nlme model combinations specified factor levels. Reports parameters estimation scale (log10) natural, back-transformed scale. Optionally includes Essential Value (EV).

title: Get Estimated Marginal Means for Demand Parameters — get_demand_param_emms
code: get_demand_param_emms(fit_obj, ...)  # Default S3 method get_demand_param_emms(fit_obj, ...)  # S3 method for class 'beezdemand_nlme' get_demand_param_emms(   fit_obj,   factors_in_emm = NULL,   at = NULL,   ci_level = 0.95,   include_ev = FALSE,   param = c("both", "Q0", "alpha"),   ... )

title: Get Estimated Marginal Means for Demand Parameters — get_demand_param_emms
text: fit_obj beezdemand_nlme object. ... Additional arguments passed emmeans::emmeans(). factors_in_emm Character vector factor names compute EMMs . Defaults factors present fit_obj. Optional named list specifying levels conditioning variables emmeans::ref_grid(). ci_level Confidence level EMMs (default 0.95). include_ev Logical. TRUE, calculates includes Essential Value (EV) derived alpha, along confidence interval (calculated back-transforming CI alpha_param_log10). Default FALSE. param Character, one "" (default), "Q0", "alpha". Controls demand parameter's EMM columns returned. "" preserves historical four-column-block structure (Q0 alpha together). "Q0" returns Q0 columns (drops EV, since EV function alpha); warning emitted include_ev = TRUE requested alongside param = "Q0". "alpha" returns alpha columns plus EV block include_ev = TRUE. Mirrors param argument beezdemand_tmb method.

title: Get Estimated Marginal Means for Demand Parameters — get_demand_param_emms
text: tibble containing: Factor levels Columns factor factors_in_emm. Q0_param_log10, alpha_param_log10 EMMs model parameters (log10 scale) respective confidence intervals (LCL_Q0_param, UCL_Q0_param, etc.). Q0_natural, alpha_natural EMMs back-transformed natural scale (10^param) respective confidence intervals (LCL_Q0_natural, UCL_Q0_natural, etc.). EV, LCL_EV, UCL_EV (include_ev=TRUE) Essential Value CI. param = "Q0" param = "alpha", columns associated requested parameter (plus factor columns , "alpha", EV block) returned.

title: Get Estimated Marginal Means for Demand Parameters — get_demand_param_emms
code: # \donttest{ data(ko, package = "beezdemand") ko$y_ll4 <- ll4(ko$y, lambda = 4) fit <- fit_demand_mixed(ko, y_var = "y_ll4", x_var = "x",   id_var = "monkey", factors = "dose", equation_form = "zben") #> Generating starting values using method: 'heuristic' #> Using heuristic method for starting values. #> --- Fitting NLME Model --- #> Equation Form: zben #> Param Space: log10 #> NLME Formula: y_ll4 ~ Q0 * exp(-(10^alpha/Q0) * (10^Q0) * x) #> Start values (first few): Q0_int=2.27, alpha_int=-3 #> Number of fixed parameters: 10 (Q0: 5, alpha: 5) get_demand_param_emms(fit) #> # A tibble: 5 × 13 #>   dose  Q0_param_log10 LCL_Q0_param_log10 UCL_Q0_param_log10 Q0_natural #>   <fct>          <dbl>              <dbl>              <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 3e-05           2.58               2.35               2.80      377.  #> 2 1e-04           2.38               2.23               2.52      238.  #> 3 3e-04           2.21               2.10               2.32      163.  #> 4 0.001           1.91               1.78               2.03       80.5 #> 5 0.003           1.90               1.73               2.07       79.7 #> # ℹ 8 more variables: LCL_Q0_natural <dbl>, UCL_Q0_natural <dbl>, #> #   alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, LCL_alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, #> #   UCL_alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, alpha_natural <dbl>, LCL_alpha_natural <dbl>, #> #   UCL_alpha_natural <dbl>  # Request only Q0 columns — convenient for pivoting and plotting get_demand_param_emms(fit, param = "Q0") #> # A tibble: 5 × 7 #>   dose  Q0_param_log10 LCL_Q0_param_log10 UCL_Q0_param_log10 Q0_natural #>   <fct>          <dbl>              <dbl>              <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 3e-05           2.58               2.35               2.80      377.  #> 2 1e-04           2.38               2.23               2.52      238.  #> 3 3e-04           2.21               2.10               2.32      163.  #> 4 0.001           1.91               1.78               2.03       80.5 #> 5 0.003           1.90               1.73               2.07       79.7 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: LCL_Q0_natural <dbl>, UCL_Q0_natural <dbl> # }

what: Calculate Descriptive Statistics by Price — get_descriptive_summary
title: Calculate Descriptive Statistics by Price — get_descriptive_summary
text: Calculates summary statistics consumption data price point, including measures central tendency (mean, median), variability (SD), range (min, max), data quality (proportion zeros, missing values). modern replacement GetDescriptives(), returning structured S3 object dedicated methods printing, summarizing, visualizing.

title: Calculate Descriptive Statistics by Price — get_descriptive_summary
code: get_descriptive_summary(   data,   x_var = "x",   y_var = "y",   id_var = "id",   by = NULL )

title: Calculate Descriptive Statistics by Price — get_descriptive_summary
text: data data frame long format columns subject ID, price, consumption x_var Character string specifying column name price (default: "x") y_var Character string specifying column name consumption (default: "y") id_var Character string specifying column name subject ID (default: "id") Optional character vector column names group . supplied, statistics computed separately within unique combination columns. Group columns prepended $statistics $data. Default NULL (grouping).

title: Calculate Descriptive Statistics by Price — get_descriptive_summary
text: S3 object class beezdemand_descriptive containing: statistics - Data frame 8 columns (Price, Mean, Median, SD, PropZeros, NAs, Min, Max) one row per unique price call - matched call data_summary - List n_subjects, n_prices, prices vector

title: Calculate Descriptive Statistics by Price — get_descriptive_summary
text: unique price dataset, function calculates: Mean - Average consumption across subjects (rounded 2 decimals) Median - Median consumption (rounded 2 decimals) SD - Standard deviation (rounded 2 decimals) PropZeros - Proportion subjects zero consumption (0-1) NAs - Count missing values Min - Minimum consumption value (rounded 2 decimals) Max - Maximum consumption value (rounded 2 decimals)

title: Calculate Empirical Demand Measures — get_empirical_measures
text: Calculates empirical (model-free) measures demand purchase task data. metrics characterize consumption patterns without fitting demand curve model. modern replacement GetEmpirical(), returning structured S3 object dedicated methods printing, summarizing, visualizing.

title: Calculate Empirical Demand Measures — get_empirical_measures
code: get_empirical_measures(data, x_var = "x", y_var = "y", id_var = "id")

title: Calculate Empirical Demand Measures — get_empirical_measures
text: data data frame long format columns subject ID, price, consumption x_var Character string specifying column name price (default: "x") y_var Character string specifying column name consumption (default: "y") id_var Character string specifying column name subject ID (default: "id")

title: Calculate Empirical Demand Measures — get_empirical_measures
text: S3 object class beezdemand_empirical containing: measures - Data frame one row per subject columns: id - Subject identifier Intensity - Consumption lowest price (demand intensity) BP0 - Breakpoint 0: first price consumption = 0 BP1 - Breakpoint 1: last price non-zero consumption Omaxe - Empirical Omax: maximum total expenditure (price × consumption) Pmaxe - Empirical Pmax: price maximum expenditure occurs call - matched call data_summary - List n_subjects, has_zeros, complete_cases

title: Calculate Empirical Demand Measures — get_empirical_measures
text: Intensity - consumption value lowest price point. Reflects unrestricted demand preferred level consumption. BP0 (Breakpoint 0) - first price consumption drops zero. consumption never reaches zero, BP0 = NA. Indicates price threshold commodity becomes unaffordable undesirable. BP1 (Breakpoint 1) - highest price consumption still non-zero. consumption zero, BP1 = NA. Represents upper limit commodity's value consumer. Omaxe (Empirical Omax) - maximum observed expenditure across prices (max price × consumption). Represents peak spending commodity. Pmaxe (Empirical Pmax) - price maximum expenditure occurs. maximum expenditure zero, Pmaxe = 0. multiple prices maximum expenditure, highest price returned.

title: Calculate Empirical Demand Measures — get_empirical_measures
text: Data must contain duplicate prices within subject (error) Missing values (NA) consumption preserved calculations applicable Breakpoints require least one zero consumption value meaningful

title: Calculate Empirical Demand Measures — get_empirical_measures
code: # \donttest{ data(apt, package = "beezdemand")  # Calculate empirical measures emp <- get_empirical_measures(apt) print(emp) #> Empirical Demand Measures #> ========================= #>  #> Call: #> get_empirical_measures(data = apt) #>  #> Data Summary: #>   Subjects: 10 #>   Subjects with zero consumption: Yes #>   Complete cases (no NAs): 6 #>  #> Empirical Measures: #>   id Intensity BP0 BP1 Omaxe Pmaxe #>   19        10  NA  20    45    15 #>   30         3  NA  20    20    20 #>   38         4  15  10    21     7 #>   60        10  15  10    24     8 #>   68        10  15  10    36     9 #>  106         5   8   7    15     5 #>  113         6  NA  20    45    15 #>  142         8  NA  20    60    20 #>  156         7  20  15    21     7 #>  188         5  15  10    15     5  # View measures table emp$measures #>     id Intensity BP0 BP1 Omaxe Pmaxe #> 1   19        10  NA  20    45    15 #> 2   30         3  NA  20    20    20 #> 3   38         4  15  10    21     7 #> 4   60        10  15  10    24     8 #> 5   68        10  15  10    36     9 #> 6  106         5   8   7    15     5 #> 7  113         6  NA  20    45    15 #> 8  142         8  NA  20    60    20 #> 9  156         7  20  15    21     7 #> 10 188         5  15  10    15     5  # Extended summary with distribution info summary(emp) #> Extended Summary of Empirical Demand Measures #> ============================================= #>  #> Data Overview: #>   Number of subjects: 10 #>   Complete cases: 6 (60.0%) #>  #> Descriptive Statistics for Empirical Measures: #> ----------------------------------------------- #>  #> Intensity: #>   Min: 3.00 #>   Median: 6.50 #>   Mean: 6.80 #>   Max: 10.00 #>   SD: 2.62 #>  #> BP0: #>   Min: 8.00 #>   Median: 15.00 #>   Mean: 14.67 #>   Max: 20.00 #>   SD: 3.83 #>   Missing: 4 (40.0%) #>  #> BP1: #>   Min: 7.00 #>   Median: 12.50 #>   Mean: 14.20 #>   Max: 20.00 #>   SD: 5.35 #>  #> Omaxe: #>   Min: 15.00 #>   Median: 22.50 #>   Mean: 30.20 #>   Max: 60.00 #>   SD: 15.40 #>  #> Pmaxe: #>   Min: 5.00 #>   Median: 8.50 #>   Mean: 11.10 #>   Max: 20.00 #>   SD: 5.88  # Visualize distribution of measures plot(emp)  # histogram by default #> Warning: Removed 4 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range (`stat_bin()`).  plot(emp, type = "matrix")  # scatterplot matrix #> Warning: Removed 4 rows containing missing values #> Warning: Removed 4 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). #> Warning: Removed 4 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range #> (`stat_density()`). #> Warning: Removed 4 rows containing missing values #> Warning: Removed 4 rows containing missing values #> Warning: Removed 4 rows containing missing values #> Warning: Removed 4 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). #> Warning: Removed 4 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). #> Warning: Removed 4 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`).  #
